frameworks.name: Next.js
frameworks.label: Next.js
frameworks.concurrency: Concurrent rendering via React Suspense + parallel RSC data fetch
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. RSC payload + per-request state in server heap
frameworks.securityPosture: Server exposed, no built-in auth, CSP manual
frameworks.whenToUse: React team, SEO, mixed static + dynamic
frameworks.whenNot: Pure static → Astro. Mobile → Expo
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Remix: bigger ecosystem, weaker form handling

frameworks.name: Remix
frameworks.label: Remix
frameworks.concurrency: Sequential loader per route — defer() for non-blocking secondary data
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Loader state per request — released after response
frameworks.securityPosture: Server exposed, web-standard fetch, no built-in auth
frameworks.whenToUse: Form-heavy apps, progressive enhancement
frameworks.whenNot: Complex data-fetching graphs → Next.js
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Next.js: simpler data model, smaller ecosystem

frameworks.name: Nuxt
frameworks.label: Nuxt
frameworks.concurrency: Sequential load() per route — ReadableStream for streaming
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Per-request server state in Nitro server heap
frameworks.securityPosture: Server exposed, Nitro server, no built-in auth
frameworks.whenToUse: Vue team, full-stack Vue apps
frameworks.whenNot: React team → Next.js
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Next.js: Vue ecosystem, smaller community
frameworks.hydration: Full — partial hydration via islands plugin

frameworks.name: SvelteKit
frameworks.label: SvelteKit
frameworks.concurrency: Sequential load() per route — ReadableStream for streaming
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Per-request state — no virtual DOM overhead
frameworks.securityPosture: Server exposed, adapter-based, no built-in auth
frameworks.whenToUse: Smallest bundle, compile-time reactivity
frameworks.whenNot: Large React team → Next.js
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Next.js: smaller bundle, smaller community
frameworks.hydration: Full — minimal overhead, no virtual DOM

frameworks.name: Angular SSR
frameworks.label: Angular SSR
frameworks.concurrency: Zone.js change detection — Signals (v17+) for fine-grained updates
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Zone.js patch tree + component tree in heap
frameworks.securityPosture: Strong DI system, CSP requires config, no built-in auth
frameworks.whenToUse: Enterprise Java-background teams, strict structure
frameworks.whenNot: Small team, fast iteration → Next.js
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Next.js: more opinionated, heavier DX overhead

frameworks.name: Solid Start
frameworks.label: Solid Start
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread async (V8) — fine-grained reactive graph, no virtual DOM diffing
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Per-request server state — fine-grained signals, lower heap than React hydration
frameworks.securityPosture: Server exposed, no built-in auth — fine-grained reactivity unusual attack model
frameworks.whenToUse: SolidJS team wanting SSR — max performance + React-like syntax
frameworks.whenNot: Large team expecting React ecosystem → Next.js
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Next.js: faster rendering via fine-grained signals, smaller ecosystem

frameworks.name: React
frameworks.label: React
frameworks.concurrency: Single render cycle — Concurrent Mode via useTransition / Suspense
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Virtual DOM fiber tree in browser heap
frameworks.securityPosture: XSS via dangerouslySetInnerHTML, no built-in auth
frameworks.whenToUse: Rich UI, large team, React ecosystem
frameworks.whenNot: SEO-critical public site → SSR
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Vue: larger community, higher learning curve

frameworks.name: Vue
frameworks.label: Vue
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread async (V8 event loop) — reactive proxy system
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Reactive proxy tree in browser heap
frameworks.securityPosture: XSS if v-html misused, no built-in auth
frameworks.whenToUse: Gentler learning curve, progressive adoption
frameworks.whenNot: Large enterprise React team → React
frameworks.tradeoff: vs React: gentler DX, smaller job market

frameworks.name: Angular
frameworks.label: Angular
frameworks.concurrency: Zone.js change detection — Signals (v17+) for fine-grained updates
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Zone.js patch tree + component tree in heap
frameworks.securityPosture: Strong DI, built-in sanitization, no built-in auth
frameworks.whenToUse: Enterprise, Java-background teams, opinionated structure
frameworks.whenNot: Small team, fast prototyping → Vue / React
frameworks.tradeoff: vs React: more structured, heavier toolchain

frameworks.name: Svelte
frameworks.label: Svelte
frameworks.concurrency: Compiler-based reactivity — no virtual DOM, no diffing
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. No virtual DOM — compiled signals, lower heap use
frameworks.securityPosture: XSS if {@html} misused, no virtual DOM attack surface
frameworks.whenToUse: Smallest bundle, compile-time reactivity, no runtime overhead
frameworks.whenNot: Large team expecting React patterns → React
frameworks.tradeoff: vs React: near-zero runtime, much smaller community

frameworks.name: Solid.js
frameworks.label: Solid.js
frameworks.concurrency: Fine-grained reactive graph — no virtual DOM, no diffing
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Fine-grained reactive graph — no virtual DOM
frameworks.securityPosture: XSS if innerHTML misused, fine-grained reactivity model
frameworks.whenToUse: Maximum runtime performance, React-like syntax
frameworks.whenNot: Large team, ecosystem maturity needed → React
frameworks.tradeoff: vs React: faster runtime, tiny ecosystem

frameworks.name: Preact
frameworks.label: Preact
frameworks.concurrency: Single render cycle — preact/compat enables React Concurrent features via compatibility layer
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. 3 kB virtual DOM — 20× smaller heap footprint than React's 45 kB runtime
frameworks.perf: TTI p99: ~0.8s on 4G (3 kB runtime vs React's 45 kB)
frameworks.securityPosture: XSS via dangerouslySetInnerHTML (React-compatible API) — same risks as React
frameworks.whenToUse: Performance-critical apps, embedded widgets, mobile web, React migration path
frameworks.whenNot: Full React ecosystem (hooks libraries, tooling) needed → React
frameworks.tradeoff: vs React: 20× smaller runtime, React-compatible via compat layer
frameworks.renderingModes: CSR only (add preact-iso or Fresh for SSR)

frameworks.name: Lit
frameworks.label: Lit
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread async (V8) — native browser custom element lifecycle, no virtual DOM
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Native Web Component lifecycle — shadow DOM per component, minimal framework heap
frameworks.perf: TTI p99: ~0.5s on 4G (6 kB runtime, native browser APIs)
frameworks.securityPosture: Shadow DOM isolation per component — XSS contained within shadow root
frameworks.whenToUse: Shareable UI components across frameworks, design systems, micro-frontends
frameworks.whenNot: Single-framework team with React/Vue chosen → use that framework's component model
frameworks.tradeoff: vs React: native browser standard — components work in any framework or vanilla HTML
frameworks.hydration: N/A — native browser custom elements, no hydration step
frameworks.runtimeTarget: Browser (framework-agnostic — works in React, Vue, Angular, vanilla)

frameworks.name: Astro
frameworks.label: Astro
frameworks.concurrency: Build-time only (Vite) — zero runtime concurrency model
frameworks.memory: GC — V8 (build only). Zero runtime heap — static files served
frameworks.securityPosture: Zero JS by default, no attack surface at runtime
frameworks.whenToUse: Content sites, docs, blogs — max performance
frameworks.whenNot: Highly dynamic data per user → SSR
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Next.js SSG: zero JS default, fewer integrations
frameworks.renderingModes: SSG primary — SSR available via adapter

frameworks.name: Eleventy
frameworks.label: Eleventy
frameworks.concurrency: Build-time only (Node.js) — zero runtime concurrency model
frameworks.memory: GC — V8 (build only). Zero runtime heap
frameworks.securityPosture: Zero JS by default
frameworks.whenToUse: Maximum control, any template language
frameworks.whenNot: React/Vue team → Astro
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Astro: simpler, no components model

frameworks.name: Hugo
frameworks.label: Hugo
frameworks.concurrency: Build-time only (Go binary) — zero runtime concurrency model
frameworks.memory: Manual (Go — no GC at build). Go binary compiled, zero runtime heap
frameworks.securityPosture: Zero JS by default, Go binary — no npm supply chain
frameworks.whenToUse: Largest site (10k+ pages), fastest build time
frameworks.whenNot: JS team, component model needed → Astro
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Astro: 100× faster builds, no JS component ecosystem

frameworks.name: Gatsby
frameworks.label: Gatsby
frameworks.concurrency: Build-time only (V8 + GraphQL layer) — React hydration in browser
frameworks.memory: GC — V8 (build only). React hydration in browser heap at runtime
frameworks.securityPosture: GraphQL layer, plugin ecosystem risks
frameworks.whenToUse: React + GraphQL data layer, image optimization
frameworks.whenNot: No GraphQL need → Astro or Next.js SSG
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Astro: GraphQL data layer, slower builds

frameworks.name: Astro
frameworks.label: Astro
frameworks.concurrency: Build-time (Vite) + island-per-component lazy hydration — no shared runtime state
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Per-island heap only — static HTML areas have zero runtime overhead
frameworks.bundleSize: ~0 kb baseline + island size per island
frameworks.securityPosture: Per-island isolation, zero JS surfaces by default
frameworks.whenToUse: Content-heavy sites needing selective interactivity
frameworks.whenNot: Fully dynamic app → SvelteKit or Next.js
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Next.js: zero-JS baseline, islands only hydrate on demand

frameworks.name: Fresh
frameworks.label: Fresh
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread async (Deno / V8) — island components hydrate independently
frameworks.memory: GC — V8 (Deno). Preact component tree per island
frameworks.securityPosture: No npm by default — smaller supply chain surface
frameworks.whenToUse: Deno-native stack, edge-first, islands model
frameworks.whenNot: npm ecosystem needed → Astro
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Astro: Deno-native, smaller ecosystem

frameworks.name: Qwik
frameworks.label: Qwik
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread async (V8) + lazy-loaded event handlers per component
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Serialized state in HTML — minimal JS heap at load, lazy loaded
frameworks.perf: TTI p99: <100ms regardless of app size
frameworks.bundleSize: <1 kb initial (lazy loads on first interaction)
frameworks.securityPosture: Serialized closures — unusual attack model, no built-in auth
frameworks.whenToUse: Large apps where hydration cost is a performance bottleneck
frameworks.whenNot: Small app where hydration cost is negligible → React / Svelte
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Next.js: near-zero TTI, steep mental model shift
frameworks.hydration: Resumable — not hydration. <1 kb JS on load regardless of app size

frameworks.name: Next.js Edge Runtime
frameworks.label: Next.js Edge Runtime
frameworks.concurrency: V8 Isolate — no shared memory between requests, event loop per isolate
frameworks.memory: GC — V8 (128 MB limit). Per-isolate heap — no shared state
frameworks.securityPosture: Reduced attack surface (no Node.js), no fs/crypto
frameworks.whenToUse: Personalized pages needing global low latency
frameworks.whenNot: Node.js APIs needed → Node.js runtime
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Node.js runtime: 10× faster TTFB, 128 MB limit
frameworks.renderingModes: SSR (edge) — pages opt-in via export runtime='edge'

frameworks.name: Hono
frameworks.label: Hono
frameworks.concurrency: V8 Isolate (Cloudflare) or Single-thread async (Deno / Bun / Node)
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Near-zero framework heap — no class instantiation
frameworks.securityPosture: Minimal surface — no framework magic, web-standard APIs only
frameworks.whenToUse: Ultra-light API or SSR at the edge
frameworks.whenNot: Full-stack app with complex routing → Next.js
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Next.js: lighter, multi-runtime, no React
frameworks.hydration: None (HTML only) or full if JSX used
frameworks.runtimeTarget: Cloudflare Workers, Deno Deploy, Bun, Node.js

frameworks.name: Remix (Cloudflare)
frameworks.label: Remix (Cloudflare)
frameworks.concurrency: V8 Isolate — web-standard fetch, no Node.js event loop
frameworks.memory: GC — V8 (128 MB limit). Loader state per request
frameworks.securityPosture: Web-standard fetch, no Node.js leakage at edge
frameworks.whenToUse: Form-heavy apps needing global low latency
frameworks.whenNot: Node.js-specific packages needed → Node.js adapter
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Next.js edge: web-standard model, Cloudflare-native

frameworks.name: Next.js App Router
frameworks.label: Next.js App Router
frameworks.concurrency: Concurrent rendering via React Suspense + parallel RSC data fetch — streaming via ReadableStream
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. React Suspense boundary state per request
frameworks.securityPosture: Server components isolated, no client exposure of server code
frameworks.whenToUse: Large pages where some sections load slower than others
frameworks.whenNot: Simple page → SSG
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Pages Router: streaming + RSC, steeper migration
frameworks.renderingModes: SSR streaming — Suspense boundaries define chunks

workflows.name: 03 PR Build + Scan — Next.js App Router 16
04 Build Push Sign — Next.js App Router 16

frameworks.name: Remix
frameworks.label: Remix
frameworks.concurrency: Sequential loader per route — defer() wraps slow loaders for streaming
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Loader state — deferred promises released after response
frameworks.securityPosture: Web-standard response streaming
frameworks.whenToUse: Deferred non-critical data, fast initial render
frameworks.whenNot: Simple page → SSG
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Next.js: simpler defer model, smaller ecosystem
frameworks.renderingModes: SSR streaming — defer() wraps slow loaders

frameworks.name: SvelteKit
frameworks.label: SvelteKit
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread async + ReadableStream — streaming via native Node.js stream
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. No virtual DOM — streaming state released per chunk
frameworks.securityPosture: No virtual DOM — smaller runtime attack surface
frameworks.whenToUse: Smallest streamed bundle, Svelte ecosystem
frameworks.whenNot: React team → Next.js
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Next.js: smaller bundle, React incompatible

frameworks.name: Module Federation
frameworks.label: Module Federation
frameworks.concurrency: Browser event loop per remote — each remote loads independently
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Each remote has own module heap — shared dependencies deduped
frameworks.securityPosture: Remote code injection risk — must validate remote origins
frameworks.whenToUse: Multiple teams, independent deployments, shared dependencies
frameworks.whenNot: Single team, small app → monorepo SPA
frameworks.tradeoff: vs iframes: shared state possible, harder to isolate

workflows.name: 03 PR Build + Scan — Module Federation Webpack 5
04 Build Push Sign — Module Federation Webpack 5

frameworks.name: Single-spa
frameworks.label: Single-spa
frameworks.concurrency: Browser event loop — orchestrator pattern, single active app at a time
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Shared global scope — all apps share one browser heap
frameworks.securityPosture: Shared global scope — XSS in one app affects all
frameworks.whenToUse: Framework-agnostic composition (React + Vue + Angular together)
frameworks.whenNot: All apps same framework → Module Federation
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Module Federation: framework agnostic, more configuration

frameworks.name: Rspack (Module Fed. v2)
frameworks.label: Rspack (Module Fed. v2)
frameworks.concurrency: Browser event loop per remote — same composition model as Webpack 5 Module Federation
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Each remote has own module heap — shared dependencies deduped via Module Federation v2
frameworks.perf: 2–10× faster builds than Webpack 5 (Rust-based compiler — same runtime overhead as Webpack)
frameworks.bundleSize: Shell ~10 kb + each remote independently (identical to Webpack 5 Module Federation)
frameworks.securityPosture: Remote code injection risk — same as Webpack Module Federation; must validate remote origins
frameworks.scaling: Horizontal (each remote deployed and scaled independently)
frameworks.whenToUse: Module Federation teams hitting slow Webpack 5 build times — Rspack is a drop-in replacement with 2–10× faster builds
frameworks.whenNot: Build speed not a bottleneck → stay on Webpack 5 Module Federation
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Webpack 5 Module Federation: identical runtime behavior and config API, 2–10× faster builds due to Rust implementation
frameworks.renderingModes: CSR (primary) — SSR support via Module Federation v2 SSR mode

workflows.name: 03 PR Build + Scan — Module Federation Rspack 1
04 Build Push Sign — Module Federation Rspack 1

frameworks.name: React Native
frameworks.label: React Native
frameworks.concurrency: JS thread + native UI thread — new arch: JSI near-synchronous bridge
frameworks.memory: GC — Hermes (Meta's JS engine). Pre-compiled bytecode — faster startup than V8
frameworks.securityPosture: JS bridge — XSS not applicable, native module risks
frameworks.whenToUse: React team targeting iOS + Android, large native module ecosystem
frameworks.whenNot: High-performance games or 3D → Flutter / Native
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Flutter: larger ecosystem, slower UI than Flutter
frameworks.stateInit: Redux / Zustand / Jotai initialized in App.tsx before first render

frameworks.name: Expo
frameworks.label: Expo
frameworks.concurrency: JS thread + native thread (React Native — same arch) — managed via Expo modules
frameworks.memory: GC — Hermes. Same as React Native + Expo module heap
frameworks.securityPosture: Managed workflow limits native attack surface
frameworks.whenToUse: React Native apps without Xcode/Android Studio setup
frameworks.whenNot: Deep native customization → bare React Native
frameworks.tradeoff: vs bare React Native: faster DX, less native control
frameworks.rendering: Native UI components — React Native under the hood
frameworks.stateInit: Same as React Native + expo-modules state via Expo managed workflow

frameworks.name: Ionic
frameworks.label: Ionic
frameworks.concurrency: Browser event loop (WebView) — JS-to-native bridge for Capacitor plugins
frameworks.memory: GC — V8 (WebView). Full web stack heap in native shell
frameworks.securityPosture: WebView — full web attack surface in a native shell
frameworks.whenToUse: Web team, web-only UI components, Capacitor plugins
frameworks.whenNot: Native performance critical → React Native / Flutter
frameworks.tradeoff: vs React Native: web-native, lower performance ceiling
frameworks.stateInit: Web framework (React/Vue/Angular) state initialized in WebView DOM context

frameworks.name: Flutter
frameworks.label: Flutter
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread (Dart isolate) + isolates for background — Impeller GPU render thread
frameworks.memory: GC — Dart VM. Generational GC per isolate — each isolate has own heap, no global pause
frameworks.perf: ~120fps (Impeller renderer — GPU direct on supported hardware)
frameworks.securityPosture: No WebView — smaller attack surface, custom renderer
frameworks.whenToUse: Pixel-perfect cross-platform UI, animations, games
frameworks.whenNot: JS team, web output primary → React Native + Expo
frameworks.tradeoff: vs React Native: faster/smoother UI, Dart is a new language
frameworks.rendering: Custom GPU renderer (Impeller — draws every pixel directly)

frameworks.name: .NET MAUI
frameworks.label: .NET MAUI
frameworks.concurrency: Thread pool + async/await — native UI thread per platform
frameworks.memory: GC — CLR (Mobile GC). Xamarin-derived mobile GC. Conservative collection
frameworks.securityPosture: .NET runtime, no WebView, native controls
frameworks.whenToUse: .NET / C# team targeting iOS, Android, Windows, macOS
frameworks.whenNot: No .NET team → Flutter
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Flutter: C# ecosystem, lower UI flexibility
frameworks.stateInit: App.xaml.cs OnStart() — dependency injection container initialized before first screen

frameworks.name: Kotlin Multiplatform
frameworks.label: Kotlin Multiplatform
frameworks.concurrency: Coroutines — multiplatform. Native coroutine support on iOS and Android
frameworks.memory: GC — JVM ART (Android) / experimental K/N GC (iOS). JVM ART: AOT compiled GC
frameworks.securityPosture: Kotlin Native on iOS — smaller attack surface than JVM
frameworks.whenToUse: Kotlin Android team adding iOS support, shared business logic
frameworks.whenNot: UI sharing needed → Flutter
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Flutter: shares logic not UI, Kotlin native on iOS
frameworks.rendering: Native UI per platform (Compose Multiplatform for shared UI)
frameworks.stateInit: ViewModel + StateFlow initialized in Activity.onCreate() (Android) / App entry point (iOS)

frameworks.name: Swift / SwiftUI
frameworks.label: Swift / SwiftUI
frameworks.concurrency: GCD + Swift structured concurrency (async/await) — actors for shared mutable state
frameworks.memory: ARC (Automatic Reference Counting). No GC pauses. Memory freed when last reference drops
frameworks.bundleSize: ~5–30 MB IPA (app-dependent, App Store thins per-device)
frameworks.securityPosture: App Sandbox, keychain, biometrics, no WebView unless needed
frameworks.whenToUse: iOS-first product, full platform API access, latest iOS features
frameworks.whenNot: iOS + Android both needed → Flutter or React Native
frameworks.tradeoff: vs React Native: best iOS perf, iOS-only
frameworks.stateInit: @StateObject + @AppStorage initialized at App struct launch. @EnvironmentObject injected at root.
frameworks.runtimeTarget: iOS / iPadOS / macOS / watchOS / tvOS / visionOS

frameworks.name: Objective-C (UIKit)
frameworks.label: Objective-C (UIKit)
frameworks.concurrency: GCD + NSOperationQueue — main thread for all UI, background queues for async work
frameworks.memory: Manual (MRC) or ARC. ARC is standard since 2011 — same model as Swift
frameworks.perf: Native — same as Swift (Core Animation / Metal)
frameworks.securityPosture: Same as Swift sandbox model
frameworks.whenToUse: Maintaining existing Obj-C codebase
frameworks.whenNot: New iOS project → Swift
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Swift: no type safety, verbose syntax, no modern concurrency

frameworks.name: Kotlin + Jetpack Compose
frameworks.label: Kotlin + Jetpack Compose
frameworks.concurrency: Coroutines + Flow — main dispatcher for UI, IO/Default dispatchers for async
frameworks.memory: GC — JVM ART. Android Runtime with AOT compilation — faster than Dalvik
frameworks.securityPosture: App Sandbox, Keystore, biometrics
frameworks.whenToUse: Android-first product, Google ecosystem, modern UI
frameworks.whenNot: iOS + Android both needed → Flutter or React Native
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Flutter: native Android perf, Android-only
frameworks.stateInit: ViewModel + StateFlow initialized in Activity.onCreate() via Hilt or Koin DI

frameworks.name: Java Android SDK
frameworks.label: Java Android SDK
frameworks.concurrency: Thread pool + Java executors — main thread for UI (AsyncTask deprecated)
frameworks.memory: GC — JVM ART. Same ART runtime as Kotlin
frameworks.securityPosture: Same Android sandbox model
frameworks.whenToUse: Maintaining existing Java Android codebase
frameworks.whenNot: New Android project → Kotlin + Compose
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Kotlin: no null safety, verbose, no coroutines
frameworks.stateInit: Variables initialized in Application.onCreate() and Activity.onCreate()

frameworks.name: Workbox
frameworks.label: Workbox
frameworks.concurrency: Service Worker (browser background thread) — intercepts fetch, serves from cache
frameworks.memory: GC — V8 (Service Worker). Isolated heap — SW failure does not crash the page
frameworks.perf: Cache hit: <10ms TTFB from SW cache. Cache miss: network-dependent
frameworks.securityPosture: HTTPS required, Service Worker scope isolation
frameworks.whenToUse: Adding offline + caching to any existing web app
frameworks.whenNot: Deep native APIs (Bluetooth, NFC) → Native or Capacitor
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Capacitor: zero native code, limited native API access
frameworks.rendering: Any (SSR/SSG/CSR) — Workbox adds Service Worker caching layer on top
frameworks.runtimeTarget: Browser (installable on iOS Safari / Android Chrome)

frameworks.name: Vite PWA Plugin
frameworks.label: Vite PWA Plugin
frameworks.concurrency: Service Worker (browser background thread) — Workbox underneath
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Same as Workbox — isolated SW heap
frameworks.perf: Same as Workbox — SW cache hit: <10ms, miss: network
frameworks.securityPosture: HTTPS required
frameworks.whenToUse: Vite-based app needing PWA without manual Workbox config
frameworks.whenNot: Non-Vite build system → Workbox directly
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Workbox: zero-config PWA for Vite, less control
frameworks.runtimeTarget: Browser (installable on iOS Safari / Android Chrome)

workflows.name: 03 PR Build + Scan — Vite PWA Plugin 0.21
04 Build Push Sign — Vite PWA Plugin 0.21

frameworks.name: Express
frameworks.label: Express
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread event loop — middleware chain sequential per request
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Minimal — middleware chain, no framework heap overhead
frameworks.securityPosture: No built-in security — needs helmet.js, cors, rate-limit
frameworks.whenToUse: Largest middleware ecosystem, maximum flexibility
frameworks.whenNot: High throughput → Fastify
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Fastify: 2× slower, larger middleware ecosystem

frameworks.name: Fastify
frameworks.label: Fastify
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread event loop — schema-validated async hook pipeline
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Schema cache (~5MB at boot). Request objects pooled
frameworks.securityPosture: Schema validation built-in (Ajv), helmet recommended
frameworks.whenToUse: High throughput JSON APIs, schema-first design
frameworks.whenNot: Non-JSON protocols or Express plugins needed → Express
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Express: 2× faster, smaller middleware ecosystem

frameworks.name: NestJS
frameworks.label: NestJS
frameworks.concurrency: DI-scoped async handlers — request pipeline via guards + interceptors
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. DI container + module graph held at boot (~30MB)
frameworks.securityPosture: Guards, interceptors, pipes — structured auth model
frameworks.whenToUse: Enterprise, Angular-background team, opinionated structure
frameworks.whenNot: Simple API → Express or Fastify
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Express: structured DI + modules, heavier boilerplate

frameworks.name: Hono
frameworks.label: Hono
frameworks.concurrency: Multi-runtime async — same handler runs on Node / Deno / Bun / CF Workers
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Near-zero heap — no class instantiation
frameworks.securityPosture: Minimal surface — web-standard APIs only
frameworks.whenToUse: Edge + multi-runtime API (Node, Deno, Bun, Cloudflare)
frameworks.whenNot: Node.js-only middleware ecosystem needed → Fastify
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Fastify: multi-runtime, no Node.js-specific middleware

frameworks.name: Deno
frameworks.label: Deno
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread async + Workers API for true parallelism
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Permissions state + module cache
frameworks.securityPosture: Permissions model — deny-by-default filesystem, network, env access
frameworks.whenToUse: Security-first server, TypeScript without build step
frameworks.whenNot: npm-heavy codebase → Node.js
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Node.js: deny-by-default security, smaller npm compat

frameworks.name: Elysia
frameworks.label: Elysia
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread async — Bun runtime only, JavaScriptCore event loop
frameworks.memory: GC — JavaScriptCore (Bun). Near-zero heap — value-based routing, no class overhead
frameworks.perf: p99: ~1ms (Bun runtime, consistently fastest TS framework)
frameworks.securityPosture: End-to-end type safety via Eden treaty — type errors at compile time
frameworks.whenToUse: Maximum Bun throughput, end-to-end TypeScript type safety, Eden RPC
frameworks.whenNot: Non-Bun runtime required → Hono
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Hono: Bun-only, higher throughput, end-to-end type safety
frameworks.threadModel: Single-thread async (Bun only — not Node/Deno compatible)

frameworks.name: Bun (native)
frameworks.label: Bun (native)
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread async — JavaScriptCore event loop (not V8), Bun.serve() built-in HTTP
frameworks.memory: GC — JavaScriptCore (JSC). Near-zero framework heap — no middleware chain, no DI container
frameworks.perf: p99: ~1ms (Bun.serve() — consistently fastest JS HTTP baseline)
frameworks.securityPosture: Minimal surface — no middleware magic, Bun's built-in TLS support
frameworks.whenToUse: Maximum raw Bun throughput, Bun-first teams, ultra-low latency APIs without framework overhead
frameworks.whenNot: npm ecosystem compatibility critical or multi-runtime required → Hono or Fastify
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Elysia: raw Bun.serve() — no framework overhead, no type safety layer — lower DX, higher throughput
frameworks.threadModel: Single-thread async (Bun / JavaScriptCore — not Node.js or Deno compatible)

frameworks.name: FastAPI
frameworks.label: FastAPI
frameworks.concurrency: Async (asyncio / Starlette) — ASGI interface, supports WebSockets
frameworks.memory: GC — CPython refcount + cycle collector. Pydantic model cache at boot
frameworks.securityPosture: Automatic OpenAPI, Pydantic validation, no built-in auth
frameworks.whenToUse: ML/AI APIs, async Python, auto OpenAPI docs
frameworks.whenNot: CPU-bound processing → Go or Rust
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Django: async-first, lighter, no ORM built-in

frameworks.name: Django
frameworks.label: Django
frameworks.concurrency: Sync (WSGI) + async (ASGI) — both supported; ORM is sync-first
frameworks.memory: GC — CPython. ORM + settings + URL resolver held in heap at boot
frameworks.securityPosture: CSRF protection, SQL injection via ORM, security middleware built-in
frameworks.whenToUse: Full-stack web app, admin panel, ORM-heavy projects
frameworks.whenNot: Lightweight API → FastAPI
frameworks.tradeoff: vs FastAPI: batteries-included ORM + admin, slower async

frameworks.name: Flask
frameworks.label: Flask
frameworks.concurrency: Sync (WSGI) + async via Quart fork — explicit per-request context
frameworks.memory: GC — CPython. Near-zero overhead — explicit per-request context
frameworks.securityPosture: Minimal — no CSRF by default, needs Flask-Login, Flask-WTF
frameworks.whenToUse: Micro-services, simple APIs, teaching Python web dev
frameworks.whenNot: Async-first → FastAPI
frameworks.tradeoff: vs FastAPI: no async, no auto-docs, more control

frameworks.name: Starlette
frameworks.label: Starlette
frameworks.concurrency: Async (asyncio) — ASGI interface. FastAPI is built on top of Starlette
frameworks.memory: GC — CPython. Minimal — FastAPI is built on top of this
frameworks.securityPosture: Minimal — FastAPI is built on top of this
frameworks.whenToUse: Raw ASGI performance, building frameworks on top of
frameworks.whenNot: Need auto-docs or validation → FastAPI
frameworks.tradeoff: vs FastAPI: lower-level, faster, no Pydantic

frameworks.name: Gin
frameworks.label: Gin
frameworks.concurrency: Goroutine per request — M:N scheduler across all CPU cores
frameworks.memory: GC — Go tri-color concurrent. Static binary ~10MB RSS per 1k goroutines
frameworks.securityPosture: No built-in auth — explicit middleware for CORS, auth
frameworks.whenToUse: High-throughput REST APIs, Go ecosystem, large community
frameworks.whenNot: Ultra-low latency → Fiber
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Echo: larger community, similar performance
frameworks.threadModel: Goroutines — M:N scheduling (OS threads backing goroutine pool)

frameworks.name: Echo
frameworks.label: Echo
frameworks.concurrency: Goroutine per request — M:N scheduler across all CPU cores
frameworks.memory: GC — Go tri-color concurrent. Static binary ~10MB RSS per 1k goroutines
frameworks.securityPosture: Same as Gin — explicit middleware
frameworks.whenToUse: Cleaner API than Gin, middleware-first design
frameworks.whenNot: Need Gin's larger plugin ecosystem → Gin
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Gin: cleaner API, smaller community

frameworks.name: Fiber
frameworks.label: Fiber
frameworks.concurrency: Goroutine per request — fasthttp (bypasses net/http, different pooling)
frameworks.memory: GC — Go tri-color concurrent. fasthttp pools request objects — lower allocation rate
frameworks.securityPosture: Deviates from net/http — middleware compatibility issues
frameworks.whenToUse: Maximum Go HTTP throughput
frameworks.whenNot: net/http middleware compatibility needed → Gin or Echo
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Gin: 2× faster, incompatible with net/http middleware

frameworks.name: Chi
frameworks.label: Chi
frameworks.concurrency: Goroutine per request — net/http compatible, M:N scheduler
frameworks.memory: GC — Go tri-color concurrent. Static binary — minimal overhead
frameworks.securityPosture: net/http compatible — all standard lib middleware works
frameworks.whenToUse: Standard library compatibility, minimal abstraction over net/http
frameworks.whenNot: Need rich middleware ecosystem → Gin
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Gin: closer to stdlib, less opinionated router

frameworks.name: Spring Boot
frameworks.label: Spring Boot
frameworks.concurrency: Thread pool (MVC, blocking) or Reactor event loop (WebFlux, reactive)
frameworks.memory: GC — JVM G1GC / ZGC. IoC container ~200MB heap at boot
frameworks.securityPosture: Spring Security built-in, CSRF, OAuth2, mature ecosystem
frameworks.whenToUse: Enterprise Java, existing Spring ecosystem, security-critical apps
frameworks.whenNot: Low startup time → Quarkus native
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Quarkus: bigger ecosystem, slower startup
frameworks.threadModel: Thread pool (MVC) or event loop (WebFlux / Netty)

frameworks.name: Quarkus
frameworks.label: Quarkus
frameworks.concurrency: Vert.x reactive event loop + worker thread pool for blocking ops
frameworks.memory: GC — JVM or GraalVM native (no GC in native mode). Zero heap startup in native
frameworks.securityPosture: GraalVM native eliminates reflection attack vectors
frameworks.whenToUse: Kubernetes-native, container-first, fast startup
frameworks.whenNot: Existing Spring team → Spring Boot
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Spring Boot: 10× faster startup native, smaller ecosystem

frameworks.name: Micronaut
frameworks.label: Micronaut
frameworks.concurrency: Reactive (Netty) + async — compile-time DI, no reflection at runtime
frameworks.memory: GC — JVM or GraalVM native. Compile-time DI — no reflection heap
frameworks.securityPosture: Compile-time DI — no reflection at runtime
frameworks.whenToUse: Compile-time DI, GraalVM native, low memory
frameworks.whenNot: Spring familiarity needed → Spring Boot
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Spring Boot: no reflection, compile-time AOP

frameworks.name: Ktor
frameworks.label: Ktor
frameworks.concurrency: Coroutine per request — Netty event loop underneath
frameworks.memory: GC — JVM G1GC. Coroutine context objects per request
frameworks.securityPosture: Explicit plugin model — no magic, auth plugin required
frameworks.whenToUse: Kotlin-first server, coroutines, JetBrains ecosystem
frameworks.whenNot: Spring familiarity needed → Spring Boot Kotlin
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Spring Boot: lightweight, Kotlin-idiomatic, smaller ecosystem

frameworks.name: Spring Boot (Kotlin)
frameworks.label: Spring Boot (Kotlin)
frameworks.concurrency: Coroutines (WebFlux) + thread pool (MVC) — Spring coroutine support on WebFlux
frameworks.memory: GC — JVM G1GC / ZGC. Spring DI container + coroutine context in heap
frameworks.securityPosture: Spring Security, full mature security model
frameworks.whenToUse: Existing Spring Java team migrating to Kotlin
frameworks.whenNot: New Kotlin project, no Spring baggage → Ktor
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Ktor: Spring ecosystem, more boilerplate

workflows.name: 03 PR Build + Scan — Spring Boot Kotlin 3.4
04 Build Push Sign — Spring Boot Kotlin 3.4

frameworks.name: ASP.NET Core
frameworks.label: ASP.NET Core
frameworks.concurrency: Async/await on Kestrel thread pool — I/O threads managed by CLR
frameworks.memory: GC — CLR (Server GC). Server GC tuned for throughput over pause time
frameworks.securityPosture: Built-in auth, CSRF, HTTPS enforcement, security middleware
frameworks.whenToUse: .NET team, high-throughput APIs, Windows or Linux
frameworks.whenNot: No .NET team → Go or Node.js
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Go: comparable speed, larger enterprise ecosystem
frameworks.threadModel: Async/await on thread pool (Kestrel I/O threads)

frameworks.name: Minimal APIs (.NET 9)
frameworks.label: Minimal APIs (.NET 9)
frameworks.concurrency: Async/await on Kestrel thread pool — same as ASP.NET Core
frameworks.memory: GC — CLR. Less startup code = slightly lower initial heap
frameworks.securityPosture: Same as ASP.NET Core — built on same runtime
frameworks.whenToUse: Simple APIs without MVC overhead, microservices
frameworks.whenNot: Complex routing, controllers, auth needed → ASP.NET Core MVC
frameworks.tradeoff: vs ASP.NET MVC: less boilerplate, less structure

frameworks.name: Axum
frameworks.label: Axum
frameworks.concurrency: Tokio async executor — multi-thread, work-stealing scheduler
frameworks.memory: Borrow checker — no GC. Zero heap allocation overhead. ~5MB RSS idle
frameworks.securityPosture: Memory safety at compile time, no null, no buffer overflow
frameworks.whenToUse: Maximum safety + performance, systems-level services
frameworks.whenNot: Rapid prototyping, team unfamiliar with Rust → Go
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Actix-web: Tokio-native, simpler type model

frameworks.name: Actix-web
frameworks.label: Actix-web
frameworks.concurrency: Async (Actix actor system + Tokio) — actor-per-handler concurrency
frameworks.memory: Borrow checker — no GC. Actix actor heap per actor instance
frameworks.securityPosture: Same Rust memory safety guarantees
frameworks.whenToUse: Highest raw HTTP throughput needed
frameworks.whenNot: Simpler API preferred → Axum
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Axum: marginally faster, more complex type constraints

frameworks.name: Phoenix
frameworks.label: Phoenix
frameworks.concurrency: Actor model — one BEAM process per connection, millions concurrent
frameworks.memory: GC — per-process BEAM GC. Each BEAM process has isolated heap — no stop-the-world
frameworks.securityPosture: CSRF, XSS, SQL injection via Ecto, secure defaults
frameworks.whenToUse: Real-time apps, WebSockets, chat, IoT — massive concurrency
frameworks.whenNot: CPU-bound computation → Go or Rust
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Node.js: 10× more WebSocket connections, fault-tolerant
frameworks.threadModel: Actor model — millions of isolated BEAM processes, no shared state

frameworks.name: Rails
frameworks.label: Rails
frameworks.concurrency: Thread-per-request (Puma) — GIL limits true CPU parallelism
frameworks.memory: GC — MRI generational. Per-request object allocation reset via GC
frameworks.securityPosture: CSRF, XSS, SQL injection via ActiveRecord, strong defaults
frameworks.whenToUse: Startup speed, convention over config, prototypes, SaaS
frameworks.whenNot: High throughput → Go or Node.js
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Sinatra: full-stack batteries, slower throughput

frameworks.name: Sinatra
frameworks.label: Sinatra
frameworks.concurrency: Thread-per-request (Puma) — GIL limits true CPU parallelism
frameworks.memory: GC — MRI generational. Near-zero framework overhead vs Rails
frameworks.securityPosture: Minimal — no CSRF by default, explicit config
frameworks.whenToUse: Simple APIs, microservices, Ruby team wanting minimal framework
frameworks.whenNot: Full-stack app → Rails
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Rails: lighter, higher req/s, no conventions

frameworks.name: Laravel
frameworks.label: Laravel
frameworks.concurrency: Process-per-request (PHP-FPM) — no shared memory. Octane (Swoole / RoadRunner) for persistent process
frameworks.memory: GC — PHP refcount + cycle. Per-request memory fully reset by FPM
frameworks.securityPosture: CSRF, XSS, SQL injection via Eloquent ORM, strong defaults
frameworks.whenToUse: Full-stack PHP, large PHP community, rapid prototyping
frameworks.whenNot: High concurrent connections → Node.js or Go
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Symfony: faster DX, less flexibility

frameworks.name: Symfony
frameworks.label: Symfony
frameworks.concurrency: Process-per-request (PHP-FPM) — no shared memory between requests
frameworks.memory: GC — PHP refcount + cycle collector. Per-request memory reset
frameworks.securityPosture: CSRF, XSS, mature security component
frameworks.whenToUse: Enterprise PHP, component-based, Drupal/API Platform underneath
frameworks.whenNot: Speed of delivery primary → Laravel
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Laravel: more flexible, steeper learning curve

frameworks.name: Slim
frameworks.label: Slim
frameworks.concurrency: Process-per-request (PHP-FPM) — minimal DI overhead
frameworks.memory: GC — PHP refcount. Near-zero framework overhead — minimal boot
frameworks.securityPosture: Minimal — PSR-7 standard, explicit middleware only
frameworks.whenToUse: Micro-APIs, PHP teams wanting minimal abstraction
frameworks.whenNot: Full-stack needs → Laravel
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Laravel: 2× faster, no conventions

frameworks.name: Vapor
frameworks.label: Vapor
frameworks.concurrency: Swift structured concurrency (async/await + actors) + SwiftNIO event loop
frameworks.memory: ARC (Automatic Reference Counting). No GC pauses. ~80MB RSS idle
frameworks.securityPosture: No built-in auth — Vapor-Auth plugin, Swift type safety
frameworks.whenToUse: iOS/macOS team extending to server, ARC memory model, no GC pauses
frameworks.whenNot: Non-Swift team → Node.js or Go
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Go: ARC vs GC, smaller ecosystem
frameworks.threadModel: Swift structured concurrency + SwiftNIO event loop

frameworks.name: Hummingbird
frameworks.label: Hummingbird
frameworks.concurrency: Swift structured concurrency + SwiftNIO event loop — lighter than Vapor
frameworks.memory: ARC. Minimal framework overhead vs Vapor
frameworks.securityPosture: Minimal surface — explicit middleware only
frameworks.whenToUse: Higher throughput than Vapor, lighter dependencies
frameworks.whenNot: Rich plugin ecosystem needed → Vapor
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Vapor: faster, smaller ecosystem, newer
frameworks.threadModel: Swift structured concurrency + SwiftNIO event loop

frameworks.name: Play Framework
frameworks.label: Play Framework
frameworks.concurrency: Akka actor system + Netty event loop — reactive by default
frameworks.memory: GC — JVM G1GC. Akka actor heap — actors hold state across requests
frameworks.securityPosture: CSRF, XSS protection, mature security helpers
frameworks.whenToUse: Scala team, reactive web apps, Akka ecosystem
frameworks.whenNot: No Scala team → Spring Boot
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Spring Boot: reactive-first, Scala type system

frameworks.name: http4s
frameworks.label: http4s
frameworks.concurrency: Cats Effect fiber scheduler — purely functional green threads on JVM
frameworks.memory: GC — JVM G1GC. Cats Effect fiber stack — purely functional allocation
frameworks.securityPosture: Purely functional — no shared mutable state
frameworks.whenToUse: Pure functional Scala, Cats Effect ecosystem, type-safe HTTP
frameworks.whenNot: Team unfamiliar with FP → Play
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Play: pure FP, steeper learning curve

frameworks.name: Ring
frameworks.label: Ring
frameworks.concurrency: Thread pool (Jetty) or async (http-kit) — functional middleware composition
frameworks.memory: GC — JVM G1GC. Clojure immutable data structures reduce GC pressure
frameworks.securityPosture: Minimal — explicit middleware model, Clojure immutability reduces state bugs
frameworks.whenToUse: Clojure team, REPL-driven APIs, functional purity
frameworks.whenNot: No Clojure team → Go or Python
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Pedestal: simpler model, less structure

frameworks.name: Pedestal
frameworks.label: Pedestal
frameworks.concurrency: Async interceptor chain — each step explicit, composable, testable
frameworks.memory: GC — JVM G1GC. Interceptor context map allocated per request
frameworks.securityPosture: Interceptor chain — each step explicit, no hidden middleware magic
frameworks.whenToUse: Interceptor-based architecture, async Clojure APIs
frameworks.whenNot: Simple API → Ring
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Ring: async interceptors, more structure

frameworks.name: Drogon
frameworks.label: Drogon
frameworks.concurrency: Coroutines + multi-thread event loop (libuv-style)
frameworks.memory: Manual — smart pointers (shared_ptr / unique_ptr). No GC — deterministic allocation
frameworks.securityPosture: No memory safety guarantees — manual management, buffer overflow risk
frameworks.whenToUse: Absolute maximum HTTP throughput, HFT, game server APIs
frameworks.whenNot: Rapid development team → Go or Rust
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Rust Axum: faster raw throughput, unsafe memory model

frameworks.name: Crow
frameworks.label: Crow
frameworks.concurrency: Multi-thread async (Boost.Asio thread pool)
frameworks.memory: Manual — smart pointers. Header-only library — no separate build step
frameworks.securityPosture: Same C++ manual memory risks
frameworks.whenToUse: Simpler C++ HTTP API than Drogon, header-only
frameworks.whenNot: Absolute maximum perf → Drogon
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Drogon: simpler API, 3× lower throughput

frameworks.name: grpc-go
frameworks.label: grpc-go
frameworks.concurrency: Goroutines — M:N green threads, HTTP/2 multiplexed streams per connection
frameworks.memory: GC — Go. Per-stream goroutine stack (2 kB initial). Low GC pause — well-suited for long-lived streams
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS 1.3 built-in, mTLS native, interceptor chain for auth and tracing
frameworks.buildTool: Go (buf or protoc for proto generation)
frameworks.whenToUse: Highest-performance Go microservices, service mesh, server + client streaming
frameworks.whenNot: Browser clients without grpc-web proxy → ConnectRPC or REST
frameworks.tradeoff: vs REST: 5–10× throughput, binary protocol — not human-readable, requires proto schema
frameworks.threadModel: Goroutines (M:N green threads — auto-scales to all CPUs)

frameworks.name: grpc-java
frameworks.label: grpc-java
frameworks.concurrency: Async via Netty event loop — non-blocking I/O with Netty NIO transport
frameworks.memory: JVM heap — Netty ByteBuf pool. JVM base overhead ~100 MB heap
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via Netty SSL, mTLS, channel credentials, server interceptors
frameworks.whenToUse: Java/Spring microservices, existing JVM ecosystem, Android gRPC clients
frameworks.whenNot: Startup time critical (serverless) → grpc-go or grpc-node
frameworks.tradeoff: vs grpc-go: comparable warm throughput, higher startup overhead and container size
frameworks.threadModel: Non-blocking async (Netty NIO — boss/worker thread pool)

frameworks.name: grpcio
frameworks.label: grpcio
frameworks.concurrency: Async (grpc.aio + asyncio) — C-core underlying transport bypasses Python GIL for I/O
frameworks.memory: Python heap + C-core native memory. GIL still applies for Python handler code
frameworks.p99Latency: p99: ~1ms unary RPC (Python overhead on C-core)
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via C-core (OpenSSL), mTLS, server interceptors
frameworks.whenToUse: Python ML model serving, data science microservices, consuming gRPC APIs from Python
frameworks.whenNot: High concurrency required → grpc-go. Performance-critical server → grpc-go
frameworks.tradeoff: vs grpcio-asyncio: sync grpcio blocks GIL per handler — use grpc.aio for async
frameworks.threadModel: Async (asyncio + C-core async I/O — GIL limits handler parallelism)

frameworks.name: @grpc/grpc-js
frameworks.label: @grpc/grpc-js
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread event loop — pure JS transport, no C bindings required
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. No C-core dependency — pure JavaScript implementation
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via Node.js native tls module, mTLS channel credentials, call interceptors
frameworks.buildTool: Node.js (proto-loader for dynamic proto loading or protoc for static)
frameworks.whenToUse: Node.js gRPC clients/servers, TypeScript microservices, interop with Go/Java gRPC
frameworks.whenNot: Native binary performance → grpc-go. Complex bidirectional streaming at scale → grpc-go
frameworks.tradeoff: vs grpc-go: pure JS — 5× higher latency, same HTTP/2 protocol wire compatibility
frameworks.threadModel: Single-thread event loop (Node.js — use cluster for multi-core)

frameworks.name: grpc-dotnet
frameworks.label: grpc-dotnet
frameworks.concurrency: Async/await (Task) — Kestrel HTTP/2 server, fully async pipeline
frameworks.memory: .NET GC — managed heap. Kestrel socket buffers, low framework overhead
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via Kestrel, mTLS client certificates, ASP.NET Core auth middleware
frameworks.whenToUse: .NET microservices, ASP.NET Core APIs replacing REST, Azure workloads
frameworks.whenNot: Browser clients without grpc-web → REST. Non-.NET clients → standard gRPC + proxy
frameworks.tradeoff: vs REST (ASP.NET Core): 3× throughput, binary protocol — debugging harder without grpc-ui
frameworks.threadModel: Async/await (Thread pool — scales to all CPUs)

frameworks.name: tonic
frameworks.label: tonic
frameworks.concurrency: Async (Tokio) — zero-cost abstractions, work-stealing multi-thread runtime
frameworks.memory: No GC — deterministic allocation. Tokio async runtime near-zero overhead per task
frameworks.p99Latency: p99: ~0.2ms unary RPC (lowest latency among major gRPC implementations)
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via rustls (memory-safe, no OpenSSL CVE risk), mTLS, tower middleware interceptors
frameworks.buildTool: cargo (tonic-build for proto generation in build.rs)
frameworks.whenToUse: Lowest latency gRPC, memory safety critical, Rust microservices, embedded service mesh
frameworks.whenNot: Rapid prototyping → grpc-go. Python/Java team → use that language's gRPC impl
frameworks.tradeoff: vs grpc-go: lower latency, no GC pauses, higher compile complexity and learning curve
frameworks.threadModel: Async Tokio (multi-threaded work-stealing — scales to all CPUs)

frameworks.name: grpc-swift
frameworks.label: grpc-swift
frameworks.concurrency: Swift structured concurrency (async/await) — SwiftNIO event loop
frameworks.memory: ARC — no GC, deterministic memory management, low per-stream overhead
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via SwiftNIO SSL (BoringSSL), mTLS, interceptors
frameworks.whenToUse: iOS/macOS gRPC clients, Vapor server with gRPC, Swift server microservices
frameworks.whenNot: Non-Apple platform primary → grpc-go. Python/Java team → use their impl
frameworks.tradeoff: vs grpc-go: native iOS/macOS integration advantage, smaller Linux server ecosystem
frameworks.threadModel: Swift structured concurrency (SwiftNIO NIO event loop — multi-threaded)

frameworks.name: grpc-kotlin
frameworks.label: grpc-kotlin
frameworks.concurrency: Coroutines — suspending functions, non-blocking on Kotlin coroutine dispatcher
frameworks.memory: JVM heap — Kotlin coroutines minimal overhead vs Java threads. Netty ByteBuf pool
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via Netty SSL, mTLS channel credentials, coroutine-aware interceptors
frameworks.whenToUse: Kotlin microservices, Android coroutine gRPC clients, Spring Boot Kotlin
frameworks.whenNot: Pure Java team → grpc-java directly. Performance critical → grpc-go
frameworks.tradeoff: vs grpc-java: idiomatic Kotlin coroutines, same JVM warm throughput
frameworks.threadModel: Coroutines (Kotlin coroutine dispatcher — non-blocking on JVM thread pool)

frameworks.name: connect-go
frameworks.label: connect-go
frameworks.concurrency: Goroutines — same as net/http, compatible with gRPC + gRPC-Web + Connect protocols
frameworks.memory: GC — Go. net/http2 transport, minimal overhead — no separate gRPC runtime
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via Go stdlib, mTLS, works with standard HTTP reverse proxies (no envoy required)
frameworks.scaling: Horizontal (compatible with standard HTTP load balancers)
frameworks.buildTool: Go (buf generate for proto + connect-go plugin)
frameworks.whenToUse: gRPC + browser clients without grpc-web proxy, standard HTTP LBs, buf.build ecosystem
frameworks.whenNot: Existing envoy + raw gRPC only → grpc-go. Non-Go teams
frameworks.tradeoff: vs grpc-go: HTTP/1.1 + browser compatible without proxy, slightly lower raw throughput
frameworks.threadModel: Goroutines (M:N green threads — auto-scales to all CPUs)

frameworks.name: grpc-php
frameworks.label: grpc-php
frameworks.concurrency: Synchronous (PHP-FPM) — each request in new FPM worker, no async model
frameworks.memory: PHP GC — ZendEngine per-request. grpc C extension is C-based native memory
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via OpenSSL (grpc C-core), mTLS supported
frameworks.whenToUse: PHP services consuming gRPC APIs (GCP client libs, Shopify), Laravel gRPC clients
frameworks.whenNot: PHP as high-traffic gRPC server → use grpc-go as sidecar. Any server workload → Go/Java
frameworks.tradeoff: vs REST: binary efficiency gained at client, lost to PHP-FPM per-request overhead at server
frameworks.threadModel: Synchronous (PHP-FPM — one request per worker process)

frameworks.name: grpc-ruby
frameworks.label: grpc-ruby
frameworks.concurrency: Thread-per-RPC (MRI) — grpc gem backed by C-core runtime; GIL limits parallel Ruby handler execution
frameworks.memory: Ruby GC (MRI generational) + C-core native memory. GIL applies to Ruby handler code; C-core I/O bypasses GIL
frameworks.p99Latency: p99: ~3ms unary RPC (MRI GIL overhead on C-core)
frameworks.throughput: ~15k RPCs/s (GIL limits Ruby handler concurrency; C-core handles I/O)
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via OpenSSL (grpc C-core), mTLS, server interceptors
frameworks.whenToUse: Rails services consuming gRPC APIs, Ruby microservices in a polyglot gRPC mesh, GCP client libraries in Ruby
frameworks.whenNot: High-throughput gRPC server → grpc-go. New non-Ruby service → Go/Java
frameworks.tradeoff: vs grpc-go: Ruby GIL limits server concurrency — grpc-ruby is best suited as a gRPC client from Rails, not as a high-traffic server
frameworks.threadModel: Thread-per-RPC (MRI GIL — use multi-process for server concurrency)

frameworks.name: Apollo Server
frameworks.label: Apollo Server
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread event loop — async resolver pipeline, DataLoader for batching
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Schema cache at boot (~10 MB). DataLoader batch window per request
frameworks.securityPosture: Introspection disabled in production mode, persisted queries, depth limiting via plugins
frameworks.whenToUse: Node.js GraphQL APIs, large Apollo ecosystem (Federation, Studio, Rover), schema registry
frameworks.whenNot: Simple CRUD → REST. High throughput → gRPC. PHP/Python primary → use their impl
frameworks.tradeoff: vs REST: flexible client queries, higher server complexity — query parsing overhead per request

frameworks.name: GraphQL Yoga
frameworks.label: GraphQL Yoga
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread event loop — Fetch API compatible, runs on Node.js / Edge / Bun / Deno
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. Lightweight — no Apollo runtime overhead, fetch-native transport
frameworks.securityPosture: Persisted operations, envelop plugin system for depth limiting and auth
frameworks.buildTool: Node.js / Edge / Bun / Deno (code-first or SDL)
frameworks.whenToUse: Lightweight GraphQL on any JS runtime, edge GraphQL, The Guild ecosystem (mesh, codegen)
frameworks.whenNot: Apollo Federation at scale → Apollo Server + Federation. Complex Studio tooling → Apollo
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Apollo Server: lighter, multi-runtime, no Studio — smaller managed tooling ecosystem

frameworks.name: Strawberry
frameworks.label: Strawberry
frameworks.concurrency: Async (asyncio) — ASGI-compatible, resolvers can be async coroutines
frameworks.memory: Python heap + SQLAlchemy/Django ORM per request. GIL limits CPU parallelism
frameworks.securityPosture: Extension system for depth limiting, max complexity, persisted queries via extensions
frameworks.buildTool: Python (type annotations drive schema generation — no SDL file required)
frameworks.whenToUse: Python teams, type-annotation-driven schema, Django/FastAPI + GraphQL, Pydantic integration
frameworks.whenNot: High throughput → gRPC. JS team → Apollo. Go team → gqlgen
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Ariadne (SDL-first): code-first types feel native to Python, SDL-first gives schema portability
frameworks.threadModel: Async (asyncio ASGI — GIL limits parallel handler threads)

frameworks.name: gqlgen
frameworks.label: gqlgen
frameworks.concurrency: Goroutines — SDL-first code generation, each resolver in its own goroutine
frameworks.memory: GC — Go. Code-generated type-safe resolvers — no runtime reflection overhead
frameworks.securityPosture: Generated type-safe resolvers eliminate reflection injection risk, query complexity limits
frameworks.buildTool: Go (gqlgen generate — SDL → Go type-safe resolver stubs)
frameworks.whenToUse: Go GraphQL APIs, type-safe resolver generation, high-throughput Go microservices
frameworks.whenNot: JS team → Apollo. Python team → Strawberry. Simple REST → chi/gin REST
frameworks.tradeoff: vs graphql-go: gqlgen generates type-safe resolver code — no runtime reflection, better perf
frameworks.threadModel: Goroutines (M:N — each resolver can run in parallel goroutine)

frameworks.name: Spring for GraphQL
frameworks.label: Spring for GraphQL
frameworks.concurrency: Async (Spring WebFlux / Reactor) or blocking (Spring MVC) — annotation-driven resolvers
frameworks.memory: JVM heap — Spring context + schema registration at boot. ~150 MB heap baseline
frameworks.securityPosture: Spring Security integration, query complexity limits via instrumentation, introspection control
frameworks.buildTool: Maven / Gradle (Spring Initializr — annotation-driven, no code generation step)
frameworks.whenToUse: Java Spring teams adding GraphQL, existing Spring Boot microservices, Spring Security integration
frameworks.whenNot: New Go/Python project → use that language. Startup critical → gqlgen
frameworks.tradeoff: vs REST (Spring MVC): adds GraphQL query flexibility, higher schema maintenance overhead
frameworks.threadModel: Async reactive (Reactor — Project Loom in JDK 21 for virtual thread option)

frameworks.name: Hot Chocolate
frameworks.label: Hot Chocolate
frameworks.concurrency: Async/await (Task) — DataLoader built-in, query plan execution engine
frameworks.memory: .NET GC — DataLoader batching reduces per-field heap allocation. Schema pre-compiled
frameworks.securityPosture: Persisted queries, query complexity, depth limits, field-level authorization via middleware
frameworks.buildTool: dotnet CLI (code-first or SDL-first, no external code generation)
frameworks.whenToUse: .NET teams, schema stitching / federation, built-in DataLoader, Banana Cake Pop IDE
frameworks.whenNot: JS team → Apollo. Go team → gqlgen. Simple APIs → REST
frameworks.tradeoff: vs REST (ASP.NET): flexible queries, higher schema complexity — justified for multi-client APIs
frameworks.threadModel: Async/await (Thread pool — scales to all CPUs)

frameworks.name: graphql-ruby
frameworks.label: graphql-ruby
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread (MRI GIL) — async via Dataloader gem, Fiber-based concurrency with Async
frameworks.memory: Ruby GC — object heap per request. Rails integration adds ~200 MB baseline
frameworks.securityPosture: Query depth limiting, complexity analysis, persisted queries, field-level authorization
frameworks.buildTool: Bundler (code-first schema DSL, no proto or SDL file)
frameworks.whenToUse: Rails apps adding GraphQL, GitHub-style API (GitHub's GraphQL runs on graphql-ruby)
frameworks.whenNot: High throughput → gRPC. Python team → Strawberry. New project → gqlgen or Apollo
frameworks.tradeoff: vs REST (Rails): GitHub proved large-scale viability — MRI GIL limits single-process concurrency
frameworks.threadModel: Single-thread (MRI GIL — Puma multi-process for concurrency)

frameworks.name: async-graphql
frameworks.label: async-graphql
frameworks.concurrency: Async (Tokio) — derive macro–driven schema, zero-cost async resolvers
frameworks.memory: No GC — deterministic allocation. Derive macros generate schema at compile time, no runtime cost
frameworks.securityPosture: Query depth and complexity limits built-in, field-level auth via guards, no runtime reflection
frameworks.buildTool: cargo (derive macros — schema generated from Rust struct annotations at compile time)
frameworks.whenToUse: Lowest latency GraphQL, Rust microservices, compile-time schema validation, zero runtime overhead
frameworks.whenNot: Rapid prototyping → Apollo or Yoga. Java/Python team → use their impl
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Apollo Server: 10× throughput, no runtime overhead, high Rust compile complexity
frameworks.threadModel: Async Tokio (multi-threaded work-stealing — scales to all CPUs)

frameworks.name: ws
frameworks.label: ws
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread event loop — each connection is an EventEmitter, message events queued
frameworks.memory: GC — V8. ~10 kB per idle connection. 10k connections ≈ 100 MB heap
frameworks.securityPosture: Origin header check, per-message auth via onmessage handler, rate limiting external
frameworks.scaling: Horizontal (sticky sessions + Redis pub/sub for broadcast)
frameworks.whenToUse: Node.js real-time apps, chat, live dashboards, collaborative tools
frameworks.whenNot: 100k+ connections per node → Go gorilla or Rust tungstenite. Fan-out broadcast → Phoenix Channels
frameworks.tradeoff: vs socket.io: lower-level, no auto-reconnect or rooms — add manually or use socket.io
frameworks.threadModel: Single-thread event loop (Node.js — cluster for multi-core)

frameworks.name: gorilla/websocket
frameworks.label: gorilla/websocket
frameworks.maintainedBy: Community (archived — use nhooyr.io/websocket for new projects)
frameworks.concurrency: Goroutines — one goroutine per connection reader, one per writer
frameworks.memory: GC — Go. ~8 kB per goroutine stack. 100k connections ≈ 1.6 GB (goroutine stack overhead)
frameworks.securityPosture: Origin checking built-in, TLS via net/http, per-message token auth pattern
frameworks.whenToUse: Go real-time services, high connection count, existing gorilla/mux codebase
frameworks.whenNot: New Go projects → nhooyr.io/websocket (gorilla archived). Fan-out at scale → Phoenix
frameworks.tradeoff: vs nhooyr.io/websocket: gorilla is archived, still works — nhooyr has cleaner context API
frameworks.threadModel: Goroutines (one per connection — M:N green threads)

frameworks.name: websockets
frameworks.label: websockets
frameworks.concurrency: Async (asyncio) — coroutine per connection, ASGI server compatible
frameworks.memory: Python heap — ~15 kB per connection object. GIL limits parallel message handling
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via asyncio SSL, origin check, per-connection token auth handler
frameworks.whenToUse: Python real-time apps, ML model output streaming, asyncio services
frameworks.whenNot: High connection count → Go gorilla or Rust. Fast broadcast → Phoenix Channels
frameworks.tradeoff: vs socket.io (Python): lower-level, no rooms or events — add manually
frameworks.threadModel: Async (asyncio — GIL limits parallel handler coroutines to one CPU)

frameworks.name: Java-WebSocket
frameworks.label: Java-WebSocket
frameworks.concurrency: Multi-thread — one thread per connection (blocking) or Netty NIO (async)
frameworks.memory: JVM heap — ~30 kB per connection thread stack. 1k connections ≈ 30 MB
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via Java SSL, origin checking, per-message auth handler
frameworks.whenToUse: Java apps needing WebSocket, Android WebSocket client, existing Java codebase
frameworks.whenNot: High connection count → Netty raw WebSocket or grpc streaming. Kotlin → prefer Ktor
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Spring WebSocket (STOMP): lower-level, simpler setup, no STOMP broker routing
frameworks.threadModel: Multi-thread blocking or Netty NIO async

frameworks.name: Phoenix Channels
frameworks.label: Phoenix Channels
frameworks.concurrency: BEAM actor model — each connection is a lightweight process (~1 kB per process)
frameworks.memory: BEAM GC — per-process heap, collected independently. 1M connections ≈ 1 GB total
frameworks.max_connections: ~1M connections/node (BEAM actor model — proven at scale)
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via Cowboy, per-channel topic auth, presence tracking built-in
frameworks.scaling: Horizontal (Phoenix.PubSub — distributed pub/sub across nodes via pg adapter)
frameworks.whenToUse: Massive fan-out broadcast, 1M+ concurrent users, real-time presence, chat at scale
frameworks.whenNot: Small team unfamiliar with Elixir → ws (Node.js) or gorilla. Simple polling → SSE
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Node.js ws: 100× more connections per node, Elixir learning curve required
frameworks.threadModel: BEAM actor model (lightweight processes — massively concurrent, preemptive scheduling)

frameworks.name: tokio-tungstenite
frameworks.label: tokio-tungstenite
frameworks.concurrency: Async (Tokio) — one Tokio task per connection, no goroutine/thread overhead
frameworks.memory: No GC — ~4 kB per connection task. 100k connections ≈ 400 MB (all connections active)
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via rustls (memory-safe), per-message auth via tower middleware
frameworks.whenToUse: Lowest latency WebSocket, Rust real-time services, game servers, trading systems
frameworks.whenNot: Rapid prototyping → ws (Node.js). Fan-out at scale → Phoenix. Team unfamiliar with Rust
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Phoenix: similar connection density, lower latency — no built-in pub/sub or presence
frameworks.threadModel: Async Tokio (multi-threaded work-stealing — scales to all CPUs)

frameworks.name: SignalR
frameworks.label: SignalR
frameworks.concurrency: Async/await (Task) — Hub pattern, transport negotiation (WebSocket → SSE → Long Polling fallback)
frameworks.memory: .NET GC — Hub group state per connection. Backplane (Redis/Azure) offloads connection state
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via Kestrel, ASP.NET Core auth, per-hub method authorization, Azure SignalR Service for 100k+
frameworks.scaling: Horizontal (Azure SignalR Service backplane — no sticky sessions needed)
frameworks.whenToUse: .NET real-time apps, Blazor integration, Azure-hosted apps, enterprise with mixed transport needs
frameworks.whenNot: Non-.NET clients at scale → Phoenix or ws. Simple WebSocket → use raw WebSocket
frameworks.tradeoff: vs raw WebSocket: auto-reconnect, groups/broadcast, transport fallback — higher abstraction overhead
frameworks.threadModel: Async/await (Thread pool — scales to all CPUs)

frameworks.name: faye-websocket
frameworks.label: faye-websocket
frameworks.concurrency: Single-thread async (EventMachine) — non-blocking event loop for Ruby
frameworks.memory: Ruby GC — ~20 kB per connection object. EventMachine event loop heap
frameworks.securityPosture: TLS via EventMachine SSL, origin checking, per-connection auth hook
frameworks.scaling: Horizontal (sticky sessions + Redis pub/sub via Faye pub/sub layer)
frameworks.whenToUse: Ruby/Rails real-time features, Action Cable alternative, Rack-compatible WebSocket upgrades
frameworks.whenNot: High connection count → Go gorilla. New Ruby real-time → Action Cable (Rails built-in)
frameworks.tradeoff: vs Action Cable: lower-level, Rack-compatible, no Rails dependency — more flexible, less convenient

phases.name: Bootstrap
phases.label: Phase 0 — Bootstrap
summary: Runs once when the repository is created. Not triggered by PRs or pushes.
Output: the hard constraints (branch protection, OIDC, registry policy, Kyverno) that every other phase depends on.

phases.name: Local Dev
phases.label: Phase 1 — Local Dev
summary: Runs on the developer machine. Triggered by git commit.
Output: commit proceeds only if pre-commit hooks pass and no secret is detected.

phases.name: PR Gate
phases.label: Phase 2 — PR Gate
summary: Runs on every push to an open PR. Triggered by: pull_request event.
Output: PR is mergeable only when all 10 stages pass and a human approves.
5 security scans (SCA, SAST, License, IaC, Secrets) run in parallel. Build waits for all 5.

phases.name: Main Build
phases.label: Phase 3 — Main Build
summary: Runs on merge to main. Triggered by: push to main.
Output: signed container image in registry with SBOM attestation, SLSA provenance, and semantic version tag.
5 security scans run in parallel. Build waits for all 5. Sign depends on container scan. Attestations depend on sign.

phases.name: Image store
phases.label: Registry — Image store
summary: Immediately after Phase 3 completes. The registry holds the canonical artifact for all promotions.
Output: image digest with signature, SBOM attestation, and SLSA provenance all attached. No promotion starts until all three are present.

phases.name: 4 environments
phases.label: Promotions — 4 environments
summary: Same 5 stages across DEV, TEST, and STAGING. PROD replaces stages 4–5 with Argo Rollouts and Monitor.
Trigger: GitOps PR merged into the target environment overlay. ArgoCD or Flux reconciles.
Gate between each environment: explicit approval required. Test pass alone does not open the gate.
