regions.name: Newfoundland & Labrador
regions.privacyRegulator: OIPC NL · ATIPPA + Access to Info Act

clusters.name: EKS
clusters.label: Amazon EKS
clusters.evidence: Terraform creates the Kubernetes cluster on EKS/GKE/AKS/OpenShift. EKS uses gp3 StorageClass; ESO authenticates via IRSA.

clusters.name: GKE
clusters.label: Google GKE
clusters.evidence: Terraform creates the Kubernetes cluster on EKS/GKE/AKS/OpenShift. GKE uses pd-ssd StorageClass; ESO authenticates via Workload Identity.

clusters.name: AKS
clusters.label: Azure AKS
clusters.evidence: Terraform creates the Kubernetes cluster on EKS/GKE/AKS/OpenShift. AKS uses managed-premium StorageClass; ESO authenticates via Managed Identity.

clusters.name: OpenShift
clusters.label: Red Hat OpenShift
clusters.evidence: Terraform (Component 27) creates the Kubernetes cluster on EKS/GKE/AKS/OpenShift.

clusterComponents.name: Namespaces + ResourceQuota + LimitRange
clusterComponents.label: Namespaces + ResourceQuota + LimitRange
clusterComponents.what: Creates isolated environments (dev/staging/prod) and caps how much CPU/memory all pods in each namespace can consume.
clusterComponents.why: Without ResourceQuota, one misconfigured service can consume all cluster CPU and starve every other service. LimitRange sets default requests/limits on pods that don't specify them.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
kubectl apply -f infra/namespaces.yaml
kubectl apply -f infra/namespace-quotas.yaml
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Every other component deploys into a namespace. StorageClass quotas affect PostgreSQL and Redis. ResourceQuota is enforced alongside Kyverno require-resource-limits policy.
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: prod-quota with limits.cpu: 40 and limits.memory: 80Gi

failures.trigger: ResourceQuota missing from namespace
failures.system: A service requests unlimited CPU; scheduler grants it; starves other services
failures.userSees: Other services become unresponsive; Prometheus alerts fire for latency spikes
failures.userCan: kubectl apply -f infra/namespace-quotas.yaml; then restart starving services

decision.chosen: Decision: ResourceQuota per namespace, not per deployment
decision.rejected: Rejected: Resource limits only in Helm values — individual deployments can be changed; namespace quota cannot be bypassed by a single service

clusterComponents.name: StorageClass
clusterComponents.label: StorageClass
clusterComponents.what: Defines how Kubernetes dynamically provisions persistent disks for PVCs (PersistentVolumeClaims).
clusterComponents.why: Without a default StorageClass, any PVC stays in Pending state indefinitely. PostgreSQL, Redis, and Loki all require persistent volumes.
clusterComponents.installCommand: kubectl apply -f infra/storage-class.yaml
clusterComponents.installNote: Cloud-specific: EKS uses gp3, GKE uses pd-ssd, AKS uses managed-premium.
clusterComponents.wiredTo: PostgreSQL (CNPG), Redis (Sentinel), Loki, Tempo all claim PVCs. Must exist before any of those are installed.
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: at least one StorageClass with (default) annotation

failures.trigger: No default StorageClass exists when a PVC is created
failures.system: PVC status stays Pending; pods that depend on it stay in ContainerCreating
failures.userSees: kubectl get pods shows PostgreSQL, Redis, Loki pods stuck in ContainerCreating
failures.userCan: kubectl apply -f infra/storage-class.yaml; PVC binds automatically within 30s

decision.chosen: Decision: gp3 (EKS) / pd-ssd (GKE) — SSD-backed storage class as default
decision.rejected: Rejected: gp2 (EKS) — older generation, lower throughput, higher latency for database workloads

clusterComponents.name: RBAC — ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, ServiceAccounts
clusterComponents.label: RBAC — ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, ServiceAccounts
clusterComponents.what: Grants minimum necessary permissions to each team (dev read-only, ops deploy, CI service account) and to each service's ServiceAccount.
clusterComponents.why: Without RBAC, every team member has cluster-admin by default — a compromised CI token can delete all namespaces.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
kubectl apply -f infra/rbac/service-accounts.yaml
kubectl apply -f infra/rbac/roles.yaml
kubectl apply -f infra/rbac/bindings.yaml
clusterComponents.wiredTo: ArgoCD uses its own ServiceAccount with deploy permissions. ESO uses a ServiceAccount with secret-read permissions. Kyverno webhook uses a ClusterRole.
clusterComponents.verifyCommand: kubectl auth can-i create pods --as=system:serviceaccount:ci:ci-runner -n prod
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: no (CI runner must not create pods directly in prod)

failures.trigger: ServiceAccount missing for a component (e.g., ESO, ArgoCD)
failures.system: The component's pod starts but immediately fails API calls with 403 Forbidden
failures.userSees: ESO logs show "forbidden: User cannot get secret"; secrets never sync
failures.userCan: kubectl apply -f infra/rbac/ to recreate all roles and bindings; restart affected pods

decision.chosen: Decision: Namespace-scoped Roles over ClusterRoles wherever possible
decision.rejected: Rejected: Single ClusterRole for all components — simpler to write, but a compromised component gains cluster-wide access

clusterComponents.name: cert-manager
clusterComponents.label: cert-manager
clusterComponents.what: Automatically issues and renews TLS certificates from Let's Encrypt for every Ingress object in the cluster.
clusterComponents.why: Without cert-manager, certificates expire silently. Manual renewal across 106 services × 3 environments = 315 certificates to track manually.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm repo add jetstack https://charts.jetstack.io
helm upgrade --install cert-manager jetstack/cert-manager \
--namespace cert-manager --create-namespace \
--set installCRDs=true \
--version v1.14.0
clusterComponents.wiredTo: ingress-nginx references cert-manager ClusterIssuers. Every Ingress object with a tls block triggers a cert-manager Certificate resource. Must be installed before ingress-nginx.
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: Ready

failures.trigger: cert-manager pod crashes or ClusterIssuer is missing
failures.system: Existing certificates continue working until they expire. New certificates cannot be issued.
failures.userSees: Browser shows "Your connection is not private" after certificate expiry (90 days max)
failures.userCan: kubectl rollout restart deploy/cert-manager -n cert-manager; verify ClusterIssuer with kubectl get clusterissuer

decision.chosen: Decision: Let's Encrypt via cert-manager HTTP-01 challenge
decision.rejected: Rejected: AWS ACM / GCP-managed certificates — cloud-provider-specific, breaks portability across EKS/GKE/AKS

clusterComponents.name: ingress-nginx
clusterComponents.label: ingress-nginx
clusterComponents.what: Runs the NGINX reverse proxy that routes all external HTTP/HTTPS traffic into the cluster based on hostname and path rules.
clusterComponents.why: Without ingress-nginx, each service would need its own cloud load balancer — that is one load balancer per service × 3 environments = 315 load balancers.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm repo add ingress-nginx https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx
helm upgrade --install ingress-nginx ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx \
--namespace ingress-nginx --create-namespace \
--set controller.replicaCount=2 \
--version 4.9.1
clusterComponents.wiredTo: cert-manager provisions TLS certs for every Ingress object. ExternalDNS creates DNS records pointing to the ingress-nginx LoadBalancer IP. All 106 services create Ingress objects referencing this controller.
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: TYPE=LoadBalancer with an EXTERNAL-IP (not pending)

failures.trigger: ingress-nginx controller pods crash or the LoadBalancer IP is lost
failures.system: All external traffic to every service fails. Services still run internally.
failures.userSees: All service URLs return connection refused or timeout
failures.userCan: kubectl rollout restart deploy/ingress-nginx-controller -n ingress-nginx

decision.chosen: Decision: ingress-nginx over cloud-native load balancers (ALB, GCP LB)
decision.rejected: Rejected: AWS ALB Ingress Controller — AWS-only, requires IRSA, incompatible with GKE/AKS

clusterComponents.name: Kyverno + Policies
clusterComponents.label: Kyverno + Policies
clusterComponents.what: Policy engine that validates, mutates, and generates Kubernetes resources. Blocks pods that run as root, lack resource limits, or use unscanned images.
clusterComponents.why: Without Kyverno, every developer must manually remember to set security contexts, resource limits, and non-root users. One forgotten setting = privilege escalation vector.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno/
helm upgrade --install kyverno kyverno/kyverno \
--namespace kyverno --create-namespace \
--version 3.1.4
kubectl apply -f infra/kyverno-policies/
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Policies enforce ResourceQuota by requiring all pods to have resource limits. Trivy Operator provides image scan results that Kyverno's verify-image policy checks. Must be installed before ArgoCD deploys any workloads.
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: disallow-root-user, require-resource-limits, disallow-privilege-escalation all in READY state

failures.trigger: Kyverno webhook is unavailable when a new pod is scheduled
failures.system: If webhook is set to Fail, all pod creation is blocked. If set to Ignore, policies are skipped.
failures.userSees: kubectl describe pod shows "failed calling webhook" or pods deploy without policy enforcement
failures.userCan: kubectl rollout restart deploy/kyverno -n kyverno; check webhook config with kubectl get validatingwebhookconfiguration

decision.chosen: Decision: Kyverno over OPA/Gatekeeper
decision.rejected: Rejected: OPA Gatekeeper — requires writing Rego; steeper learning curve; no mutation support for auto-setting missing fields

clusterComponents.name: Network Policies
clusterComponents.label: Network Policies
clusterComponents.what: Kubernetes NetworkPolicy resources that restrict pod-to-pod traffic — each service can only talk to the services it explicitly needs.
clusterComponents.why: Without network policies, any compromised container can reach every database, secret store, and internal service in the cluster — flat network = complete lateral movement.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
kubectl apply -f infra/network-policies/default-deny-all.yaml
kubectl apply -f infra/network-policies/allow-ingress-nginx.yaml
kubectl apply -f infra/network-policies/allow-monitoring.yaml
kubectl apply -f infra/network-policies/per-service/
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Requires a CNI plugin that enforces NetworkPolicy (Calico, Cilium, or cloud-native VPC CNI with NetworkPolicy support). ingress-nginx must be whitelisted to reach services. Prometheus must be whitelisted to scrape metrics.
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: wget: download timed out (blocked by default-deny policy)

failures.trigger: CNI plugin does not support NetworkPolicy enforcement (e.g., flannel without policy support)
failures.system: NetworkPolicy objects are accepted by the API server but silently ignored; all traffic passes
failures.userSees: No immediate error — policies appear installed but provide zero protection
failures.userCan: Verify CNI: kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep calico. Replace CNI if needed (requires node drain).

decision.chosen: Decision: Default-deny-all base policy with explicit allow rules per service
decision.rejected: Rejected: Allow-all base with deny rules for sensitive services — too easy to miss a new service that should be restricted

clusterComponents.name: Falco
clusterComponents.label: Falco
clusterComponents.what: Runtime security tool that monitors kernel system calls inside containers and alerts when behavior matches known attack patterns (shell spawn, file read in /etc/shadow, network scan).
clusterComponents.why: Kyverno and network policies prevent misconfigurations but do nothing once a container is running and compromised. Falco is the only component that detects attacks happening inside a live container.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm repo add falcosecurity https://falcosecurity.github.io/charts
helm upgrade --install falco falcosecurity/falco \
--namespace falco --create-namespace \
--set falco.grpc.enabled=true \
--set falco.grpc_output.enabled=true \
--version 3.8.7
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Falco alerts route to Alertmanager via falco-exporter. Alertmanager sends to Slack/PagerDuty. Falco requires privileged DaemonSet access to host kernel — must coexist with Kyverno's disallow-privilege-escalation policy (falco namespace is exempted).
clusterComponents.verifyCommand: kubectl exec -n falco $(kubectl get pod -n falco -l app=falco -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- cat /var/log/falco.log | head -5
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: Falco version line + "Loading rules from file /etc/falco/falco_rules.yaml"

failures.trigger: Falco DaemonSet pod crashes (kernel version mismatch with eBPF probe)
failures.system: Runtime monitoring stops for that node; no alerts for attacks on that node
failures.userSees: No alerts — silence, not an error. Only detected by checking DaemonSet pod health.
failures.userCan: kubectl get daemonset falco -n falco; check driver compatibility at falco.org/docs; update driver version in Helm values

decision.chosen: Decision: Falco eBPF driver over kernel module
decision.rejected: Rejected: Falco kernel module — requires loading a kernel module on each node; blocked by managed Kubernetes (GKE, EKS) which disallow unsigned kernel modules

clusterComponents.name: Pod Security Admission (PSA)
clusterComponents.label: Pod Security Admission (PSA)
clusterComponents.what: Kubernetes built-in admission controller (no install required) that enforces pod security standards (restricted/baseline/privileged) per namespace via namespace labels.
clusterComponents.why: PSA is a second, independent enforcement layer. Even if Kyverno is misconfigured or temporarily down, PSA still blocks privileged pods at the API server level.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
kubectl label namespace prod pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted
kubectl label namespace prod pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=restricted
kubectl label namespace prod pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit=restricted
clusterComponents.installNote: PSA is built into Kubernetes 1.25+. No Helm chart needed.
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Works alongside Kyverno — PSA catches violations Kyverno might miss (e.g., during Kyverno pod restart). Falco namespace must be labeled privileged to allow its DaemonSet.
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted present in labels

failures.trigger: Namespace label missing — PSA not applied
failures.system: Privileged pods deploy without warning; PSA is opt-in, not global
failures.userSees: No error — the pod runs as root with no indication
failures.userCan: kubectl label namespace prod pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted --overwrite

decision.chosen: Decision: PSA restricted mode on prod, baseline on dev/test
decision.rejected: Rejected: restricted mode everywhere — too many dev-only tools (debug containers, profilers) need elevated permissions

clusterComponents.name: ArgoCD
clusterComponents.label: ArgoCD
clusterComponents.what: GitOps controller that watches Git repositories and automatically deploys changes to Kubernetes — reconciling actual cluster state with desired state in Git.
clusterComponents.why: Without ArgoCD, every deploy requires a human running kubectl apply. With 106 services × 3 environments, that is manual work on 315 deployments.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm repo add argo https://argoproj.github.io/argo-helm
helm upgrade --install argocd argo/argo-cd \
--namespace argocd --create-namespace \
--values infra/argocd/values.yaml \
--version 6.7.3
clusterComponents.wiredTo: ESO must exist before ArgoCD deploys workloads that reference ExternalSecrets. cert-manager and ingress-nginx must exist before ArgoCD deploys services with Ingress objects. GHCR pull secret must exist before ArgoCD triggers image pulls.
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: all applications show STATUS=Synced and HEALTH=Healthy

failures.trigger: ArgoCD server pod crashes or Git repo becomes unreachable
failures.system: Existing running services are unaffected. New commits are not deployed until ArgoCD recovers.
failures.userSees: ArgoCD UI unreachable; no new deploys reach the cluster
failures.userCan: kubectl rollout restart deploy/argocd-server -n argocd; verify git connectivity with argocd repo list

decision.chosen: Decision: ArgoCD over Flux v2
decision.rejected: Rejected: Flux v2 — no built-in UI, harder to debug sync failures for non-Kubernetes-expert developers

clusterComponents.name: Argo Rollouts
clusterComponents.label: Argo Rollouts
clusterComponents.what: Extends Kubernetes Deployments with canary and blue-green release strategies — sends a small percentage of traffic to the new version and auto-rolls back if error rate or latency exceeds thresholds.
clusterComponents.why: Standard Kubernetes Deployment sends 100% of traffic to the new version immediately. One bad deploy = complete outage. Argo Rollouts limits blast radius to the canary percentage.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm upgrade --install argo-rollouts argo/argo-rollouts \
--namespace argo-rollouts --create-namespace \
--version 2.35.1
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Uses Prometheus metrics to make auto-rollback decisions — requires Prometheus to be running and scraping the target service. ArgoCD deploys Rollout objects instead of standard Deployment objects.
clusterComponents.verifyCommand: kubectl argo rollouts get rollout <service-name> -n prod --watch
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: shows canary weight progression and Healthy status

failures.trigger: Argo Rollouts controller pod crashes during an active canary
failures.system: The canary stops progressing; traffic split freezes at current percentage; no auto-rollback fires
failures.userSees: Rollout appears stuck; must manually promote or abort via kubectl argo rollouts abort
failures.userCan: kubectl rollout restart deploy/argo-rollouts -n argo-rollouts; then manually promote or abort the stuck rollout

decision.chosen: Decision: Argo Rollouts canary with Prometheus analysis
decision.rejected: Rejected: Manual canary via ingress weight annotations — requires human watching metrics and manually adjusting weights; no automatic rollback

clusterComponents.name: External Secrets Operator (ESO)
clusterComponents.label: External Secrets Operator (ESO)
clusterComponents.what: Operator that watches ExternalSecret custom resources and synchronizes secrets from AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, or Azure Key Vault into Kubernetes Secret objects.
clusterComponents.why: Without ESO, secrets must be base64-encoded and stored in etcd (the Kubernetes database). etcd is not encrypted at rest by default — storing raw secrets there is a security violation.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm repo add external-secrets https://charts.external-secrets.io
helm upgrade --install external-secrets external-secrets/external-secrets \
--namespace external-secrets --create-namespace \
--version 0.9.18
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Requires IRSA (EKS), Workload Identity (GKE), or Managed Identity (AKS) to authenticate to the cloud secret store. ArgoCD deploys ExternalSecret objects. Must be installed before ArgoCD deploys any service that needs a secret.
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: all ExternalSecrets show STATUS=SecretSynced

failures.trigger: Cloud IAM permission revoked from ESO's ServiceAccount
failures.system: ESO cannot read from the secret store; existing Kubernetes Secrets retain their last-synced values; new secrets cannot be created
failures.userSees: kubectl get externalsecret shows STATUS=SecretSyncedError; new pod deployments fail if they reference the missing secret
failures.userCan: Restore IAM permission; kubectl annotate externalsecret <name> force-sync=$(date +%s) to trigger resync

decision.chosen: Decision: ESO over Kubernetes native Secrets with Sealed Secrets
decision.rejected: Rejected: Bitnami Sealed Secrets — encrypts secrets in Git, but still stores them in etcd; requires managing decryption keys per cluster

clusterComponents.name: GHCR Pull Secret
clusterComponents.label: GHCR Pull Secret
clusterComponents.what: A Kubernetes Secret of type docker-registry containing GitHub Container Registry credentials — required for the cluster to pull private container images.
clusterComponents.why: Without this secret, every pod that tries to pull an image from ghcr.io/yarova-ca/* gets ImagePullBackOff — no service can start.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
kubectl create secret docker-registry ghcr-pull-secret \
--docker-server=ghcr.io \
--docker-username=<github-username> \
--docker-password=<github-pat-with-packages-read> \
--namespace prod
# Repeat for dev and test namespaces
# Or use ESO to sync from the cloud secret store automatically
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Every pod spec references this secret via imagePullSecrets. The default ServiceAccount in each namespace must be patched to include the pull secret, or each Deployment must explicitly reference it.
clusterComponents.verifyCommand: kubectl get secret ghcr-pull-secret -n prod -o jsonpath='{.type}'
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson

failures.trigger: GitHub PAT expires or is revoked
failures.system: New pod starts trigger image pulls that fail with 401 Unauthorized; existing running pods are unaffected
failures.userSees: kubectl get events -n prod shows ErrImagePull for any pod that restarts or is newly scheduled
failures.userCan: Generate new PAT; kubectl delete secret ghcr-pull-secret -n prod; kubectl create secret docker-registry ghcr-pull-secret ... with new PAT

decision.chosen: Decision: Single shared pull secret managed via ESO, synced from cloud secret store
decision.rejected: Rejected: Per-service pull secrets — 105 secrets to rotate every time the PAT expires

clusterComponents.name: PostgreSQL — CloudNativePG (CNPG)
clusterComponents.label: PostgreSQL — CloudNativePG (CNPG)
clusterComponents.what: HA PostgreSQL cluster with 1 primary + 2 replicas running inside Kubernetes. CloudNativePG handles streaming replication, automatic failover, and connection management.
clusterComponents.why: Services need a database. Running PostgreSQL in Kubernetes with CNPG gives automated failover in under 30 seconds — critical for the 99.9% SLO.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm upgrade --install cnpg cloudnative-pg/cloudnative-pg \
--namespace cnpg-system --create-namespace \
--version 0.21.0
kubectl apply -f infra/postgres/cluster.yaml
clusterComponents.wiredTo: StorageClass (Component 2) must exist — CNPG creates PVCs for each instance. PgBouncer (Component 15) connects to the CNPG primary service. Backup (Component 25) configures WAL archival on the CNPG cluster object.
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: READY=3/3, STATUS=Cluster in healthy state

failures.trigger: Primary PostgreSQL pod crashes
failures.system: CNPG promotes one replica to primary within 30 seconds; new connections route to the new primary
failures.userSees: ~30s of database errors, then automatic recovery. Services log "connection refused" briefly then resume.
failures.userCan: Monitor with kubectl get cluster -n postgres --watch; CNPG handles failover automatically

decision.chosen: Decision: CNPG (CloudNativePG) over managed RDS/Cloud SQL
decision.rejected: Rejected: AWS RDS — managed and reliable, but cloud-provider-locked and 3× more expensive per month at production sizing

clusterComponents.name: PgBouncer — Connection Pooler
clusterComponents.label: PgBouncer — Connection Pooler
clusterComponents.what: Connection pooler that sits between all 106 services and PostgreSQL — multiplexes many service connections into a small pool of actual PostgreSQL connections.
clusterComponents.why: PostgreSQL max_connections defaults to 100. With 106 services each maintaining a connection pool, the total exceeds max_connections within minutes of startup — without PgBouncer, the database refuses new connections.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
kubectl apply -f infra/pgbouncer/deployment.yaml
kubectl apply -f infra/pgbouncer/service.yaml
kubectl apply -f infra/pgbouncer/configmap.yaml
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Services connect to PgBouncer's ClusterIP (not directly to CNPG). PgBouncer connects to the CNPG primary read-write service. Credentials stored as an ESO-managed Kubernetes Secret.
clusterComponents.verifyCommand: kubectl exec -n postgres deploy/pgbouncer -- psql -h localhost -p 5432 -U pgbouncer pgbouncer -c "SHOW POOLS;"
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: pools table showing active client and server connections

failures.trigger: PgBouncer deployment is unavailable (pod crash or misconfiguration)
failures.system: All services that connect through PgBouncer lose database access immediately; services that connect directly to CNPG are unaffected
failures.userSees: HTTP 500 errors from all services that require database reads or writes
failures.userCan: kubectl rollout restart deploy/pgbouncer -n postgres; verify with SHOW POOLS command above

decision.chosen: Decision: PgBouncer in transaction pooling mode
decision.rejected: Rejected: Session pooling mode — holds a server connection per client session; does not solve the max_connections problem under load

clusterComponents.name: Redis — Sentinel HA
clusterComponents.label: Redis — Sentinel HA
clusterComponents.what: HA Redis cluster (1 primary + 2 replicas + 3 Sentinel processes) for distributed caching, token blacklist, and rate limiting across all 106 services.
clusterComponents.why: Without a shared Redis, token revocation only works on the pod that processed the logout — other pods still accept the revoked token. Rate limiting is per-pod, not per-user-globally.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami
helm upgrade --install redis bitnami/redis \
--namespace redis --create-namespace \
--set architecture=replication \
--set sentinel.enabled=true \
--set auth.existingSecret=redis-password \
--version 18.19.4
clusterComponents.wiredTo: All 106 services connect to the Redis Sentinel endpoint (not the primary directly). Token blacklist entries written by auth-service are read by all API services. ESO syncs the redis-password secret.
clusterComponents.verifyCommand: kubectl exec -n redis redis-master-0 -- redis-cli -a <password> ping
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: PONG

failures.trigger: Redis primary pod crashes
failures.system: Sentinel promotes a replica to primary within 10–30 seconds; services briefly see connection errors then reconnect
failures.userSees: ~15s of rate limit bypass possible during failover; token revocations during this window may not propagate
failures.userCan: Monitor with kubectl get pods -n redis; Sentinel handles failover automatically

decision.chosen: Decision: Redis Sentinel over Redis Cluster
decision.rejected: Rejected: Redis Cluster — horizontal sharding; adds complexity for key routing that is unnecessary at current scale (well under 100GB keyspace)

clusterComponents.name: Prometheus + Grafana
clusterComponents.label: Prometheus + Grafana
clusterComponents.what: Prometheus scrapes metrics from all pods every 15 seconds and stores them in a time-series database. Grafana visualizes the metrics as dashboards and SLO burn rate charts.
clusterComponents.why: Without Prometheus, you cannot answer "is the service slow right now, or did it slow down 20 minutes ago?" You cannot detect an SLO breach before it becomes an outage.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts
helm upgrade --install kube-prometheus-stack \
prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack \
--namespace monitoring --create-namespace \
--values infra/monitoring/prometheus-values.yaml \
--version 57.2.0
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Argo Rollouts reads Prometheus metrics for canary analysis. Alertmanager (Component 18) routes alerts triggered by Prometheus rules. Grafana datasources include Loki (Component 19) and Tempo (Component 20) via Component 29.
clusterComponents.verifyCommand:
kubectl port-forward svc/kube-prometheus-stack-prometheus -n monitoring 9090:9090
# Then open http://localhost:9090/targets
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: all service targets show State=UP

failures.trigger: Prometheus pod runs out of disk (default 50Gi PVC)
failures.system: Prometheus stops ingesting metrics; existing data is readable until TSDB compaction fails
failures.userSees: Grafana dashboards show "No data" for recent timeframes; Alertmanager stops firing alerts
failures.userCan: kubectl get pvc -n monitoring; expand PVC or reduce retention from 15d to 7d in Helm values; restart Prometheus pod

decision.chosen: Decision: kube-prometheus-stack (all-in-one Helm chart) over installing Prometheus and Grafana separately
decision.rejected: Rejected: Separate Prometheus and Grafana installs — requires manually wiring datasources, recording rules, and default dashboards

clusterComponents.name: Alertmanager
clusterComponents.label: Alertmanager
clusterComponents.what: Receives alerts from Prometheus, deduplicates them, groups related alerts, and routes them to Slack channels or PagerDuty based on severity and namespace.
clusterComponents.why: Prometheus fires alerts into a void without Alertmanager. A disk full event at 3am is never seen until a developer checks dashboards in the morning.
clusterComponents.installCommand: kubectl apply -f infra/monitoring/alertmanager-config.yaml
clusterComponents.installNote: Alertmanager is included in the kube-prometheus-stack Helm chart (Component 17). Configure routing in values.yaml:
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Prometheus sends alerts to Alertmanager via its built-in alertmanager endpoint. Alertmanager reads a Secret containing Slack webhook URL and PagerDuty integration key (synced by ESO).
clusterComponents.verifyCommand:
kubectl port-forward svc/kube-prometheus-stack-alertmanager -n monitoring 9093:9093
# Then open http://localhost:9093/#/alerts
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: Alertmanager UI loads; check that Slack/PagerDuty receivers are listed under Status > Config

failures.trigger: Slack webhook URL rotated; old URL stored in Alertmanager config
failures.system: Alertmanager fires alerts but all Slack notifications fail with 403; PagerDuty still works if configured separately
failures.userSees: No Slack alerts; only discoverable by checking Alertmanager logs
failures.userCan: Update Slack webhook in cloud secret store; ESO syncs to Kubernetes Secret; kubectl rollout restart alertmanager pods

decision.chosen: Decision: Alertmanager routing: critical=PagerDuty, warning=Slack
decision.rejected: Rejected: All alerts to Slack — alert fatigue; critical alerts buried under warnings; no escalation path

clusterComponents.name: Loki + Promtail
clusterComponents.label: Loki + Promtail
clusterComponents.what: Loki is a log aggregation system. Promtail is a DaemonSet agent that reads logs from all pod stdout and ships them to Loki with Kubernetes metadata labels.
clusterComponents.why: Without Loki, finding what happened on a specific request across 14 microservices requires SSHing to nodes and grepping individual pod logs — impossible when pods have already restarted.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm repo add grafana https://grafana.github.io/helm-charts
helm upgrade --install loki grafana/loki-stack \
--namespace monitoring \
--set promtail.enabled=true \
--set loki.persistence.enabled=true \
--set loki.persistence.size=50Gi \
--version 2.10.2
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Promtail runs as a DaemonSet and reads /var/log/pods/* on each node. Loki stores logs and is queried by Grafana (Component 29 wires this). Structured JSON logs from services include a trace_id field that links to Tempo (Component 20).
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: "level=info msg=Sending batch" entries with non-zero byte counts

failures.trigger: Loki PVC fills up (50Gi default, 7-day retention)
failures.system: Loki stops accepting new logs; Promtail buffers briefly then drops logs
failures.userSees: Grafana log queries return no recent results; Promtail logs show "429 Too Many Requests" from Loki
failures.userCan: Expand Loki PVC or reduce retention; kubectl delete pod loki-0 -n monitoring to restart after expansion

decision.chosen: Decision: Loki over Elasticsearch/OpenSearch for logs
decision.rejected: Rejected: Elasticsearch — indexes all log fields, which is powerful but requires 8–16GB RAM per node at this log volume

clusterComponents.name: Tempo — Distributed Tracing
clusterComponents.label: Tempo — Distributed Tracing
clusterComponents.what: Distributed tracing backend that stores OpenTelemetry spans. Every request gets a trace ID; Tempo shows the full call chain across all 14 microservices it touched.
clusterComponents.why: A 500ms latency spike in prod spans 8 services. Without tracing you cannot tell which service added 400ms. With Tempo you see the exact span — 400ms was the PostgreSQL query in user-service.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm upgrade --install tempo grafana/tempo \
--namespace monitoring \
--set tempo.storage.trace.backend=local \
--set tempo.storage.trace.local.path=/var/tempo/traces \
--version 1.7.1
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Services send OTel spans to the Tempo endpoint (OTLP gRPC port 4317). Loki logs include trace_id; Grafana can jump from a log line to the matching trace. Component 29 wires Tempo as a Grafana datasource.
clusterComponents.verifyCommand:
kubectl port-forward svc/tempo -n monitoring 3200:3200
curl http://localhost:3200/api/echo
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: {"status":"ok"}

failures.trigger: Tempo pod crashes or disk fills up
failures.system: New trace spans are dropped; existing stored traces remain readable until TTL
failures.userSees: Grafana Explore shows "No data" for traces; services continue working normally
failures.userCan: kubectl rollout restart deploy/tempo -n monitoring; clear old traces if disk is full

decision.chosen: Decision: Tempo over Jaeger
decision.rejected: Rejected: Jaeger — does not integrate natively with Grafana; separate UI required; no out-of-the-box Loki log correlation

clusterComponents.name: Trivy Operator
clusterComponents.label: Trivy Operator
clusterComponents.what: Continuously scans every container image running in the cluster for CVEs and exposes scan results as Kubernetes CRDs (VulnerabilityReport) queryable via kubectl.
clusterComponents.why: CI scans images at build time. Trivy Operator scans images while they are running — a CVE published after the last build is caught within hours, not in the next quarterly security review.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm repo add aqua https://aquasecurity.github.io/helm-charts
helm upgrade --install trivy-operator aqua/trivy-operator \
--namespace trivy-system --create-namespace \
--version 0.21.4
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Scan results are exposed as Prometheus metrics (trivy_image_vulnerabilities gauge) scraped by Prometheus. Grafana dashboard shows CVE counts per image. Kyverno can reference scan results to block images with critical CVEs.
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: count equal to number of unique container images running in prod namespace

failures.trigger: Trivy Operator cannot pull the vulnerability database (air-gapped cluster or network policy blocks egress)
failures.system: Scan jobs fail; VulnerabilityReports become stale; new CVEs not detected
failures.userSees: kubectl get vulnerabilityreport shows old scan dates; kubectl describe vulnerabilityreport shows "failed to download DB"
failures.userCan: Mirror Trivy DB to internal registry; configure operator to use mirror via Helm values trivyOperator.trivyImageRepository

decision.chosen: Decision: Trivy Operator (cluster-side continuous scan) in addition to CI-time Trivy scan
decision.rejected: Rejected: CI-only scanning — CVEs published after build are invisible until next build cycle, which can be weeks for stable services

clusterComponents.name: Velero — Cluster Backup
clusterComponents.label: Velero — Cluster Backup
clusterComponents.what: Backs up all Kubernetes resource definitions (Deployments, ConfigMaps, Secrets, CRDs, RBAC) and PVC snapshots to S3/GCS. Enables full cluster restore from scratch.
clusterComponents.why: Without Velero, a catastrophic cluster failure (accidental deletion, cloud provider incident) requires manually recreating every resource. With Velero, restore is one command and takes under 30 minutes.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm repo add vmware-tanzu https://vmware-tanzu.github.io/helm-charts
helm upgrade --install velero vmware-tanzu/velero \
--namespace velero --create-namespace \
--values infra/velero/values.yaml \
--version 6.4.0
# values.yaml sets S3 bucket, schedule: "0 2 * * *", TTL: 720h (30 days)
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Requires cloud IAM permissions to write to S3/GCS (via IRSA/Workload Identity). PVC snapshots use the cloud provider's snapshot API. ESO-synced secrets are backed up as Kubernetes Secret objects (already pulled from the cloud store).
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: most recent backup with STATUS=Completed and ERRORS=0

failures.trigger: Velero backup job fails (S3 permission error, disk snapshot quota hit)
failures.system: The cluster continues running; only the backup copy is missing; previous backup is still valid
failures.userSees: velero backup get shows STATUS=Failed; Alertmanager fires VeleroBackupFailed alert
failures.userCan: velero backup describe <name> --details to see error; fix IAM permission or quota; velero backup create manual-backup to retry

decision.chosen: Decision: Velero daily backup at 02:00 UTC, 30-day retention
decision.rejected: Rejected: Hourly backups — EBS/PD snapshot costs scale linearly; 30 snapshots per PVC per day at prod scale exceeds $500/month

clusterComponents.name: ExternalDNS
clusterComponents.label: ExternalDNS
clusterComponents.what: Watches Ingress objects and automatically creates/updates DNS A records in Route53/Cloud DNS/Azure DNS pointing to the ingress-nginx LoadBalancer IP.
clusterComponents.why: Without ExternalDNS, deploying a new service requires a manual DNS entry. With 106 services × 3 environments, that is 315 DNS entries to keep synchronized manually.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm repo add external-dns https://kubernetes-sigs.github.io/external-dns/
helm upgrade --install external-dns external-dns/external-dns \
--namespace external-dns --create-namespace \
--set provider=aws \
--set policy=upsert-only \
--set txtOwnerId=prod-cluster \
--version 1.14.4
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Reads hostname from Ingress spec.rules[].host. Creates DNS records pointing to the ingress-nginx LoadBalancer external IP. Requires Route53 write permissions via IRSA (EKS) or equivalent.
clusterComponents.verifyCommand: kubectl logs -n external-dns deploy/external-dns | grep "Desired change"
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: "Desired change: 0" when DNS is in sync with Ingress objects

failures.trigger: ExternalDNS IAM permission revoked
failures.system: Existing DNS records remain valid; new Ingress objects do not get DNS entries; deleted services keep their DNS records
failures.userSees: New service URL returns NXDOMAIN; ExternalDNS logs show "AccessDenied" from Route53
failures.userCan: Restore IAM permission; ExternalDNS will reconcile on next sync cycle (default 1 minute)

decision.chosen: Decision: policy=upsert-only (never deletes DNS records)
decision.rejected: Rejected: policy=sync — automatically deletes DNS records for removed Ingresses; one accidental Ingress deletion = prod URL goes dark

clusterComponents.name: Cluster Autoscaler
clusterComponents.label: Cluster Autoscaler
clusterComponents.what: Monitors pods in Pending state due to insufficient node resources and automatically adds nodes to the cluster; removes underutilized nodes during low traffic periods.
clusterComponents.why: Without the autoscaler, node capacity must be over-provisioned to handle peak load — wasting money at off-peak hours. Or nodes run full and new pods stay Pending indefinitely during traffic spikes.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm repo add autoscaler https://kubernetes.github.io/autoscaler
helm upgrade --install cluster-autoscaler autoscaler/cluster-autoscaler \
--namespace kube-system \
--set autoDiscovery.clusterName=prod \
--set awsRegion=us-east-1 \
--version 9.36.0
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Reads ResourceQuota and node taints to calculate whether a pending pod can fit on a new node. Node groups must have autoscaling enabled in the cloud provider (ASG on EKS, MIG on GKE). ResourceQuota caps still apply — autoscaler cannot add capacity beyond namespace quota.
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: scale-up events matching traffic spikes; or "No scale up needed" during normal load

failures.trigger: Node group max size reached; cluster cannot add more nodes
failures.system: Pods remain in Pending state; existing services are unaffected; autoscaler logs "max node group size reached"
failures.userSees: New deployments or scaled replicas stay Pending; kubectl get pods shows Pending with "0/N nodes are available"
failures.userCan: Increase node group max size in cloud console or Terraform; autoscaler detects the higher limit within 10 minutes

decision.chosen: Decision: Cluster Autoscaler over Karpenter (EKS)
decision.rejected: Rejected: Karpenter — more flexible instance selection and faster provisioning, but EKS-only; incompatible with the multi-cloud cluster design

clusterComponents.name: PostgreSQL Backup (CNPG WAL Archival)
clusterComponents.label: PostgreSQL Backup (CNPG WAL Archival)
clusterComponents.what: Configures CloudNativePG to continuously archive WAL (Write-Ahead Log) segments to S3/GCS and take daily base backups — enabling point-in-time recovery (PITR) to any second in the last 7 days.
clusterComponents.why: Velero backs up Kubernetes resources. PostgreSQL data lives inside PVCs and is not safely backed up by volume snapshots alone — a snapshot mid-transaction produces a corrupted backup. WAL archival is the only correct PostgreSQL backup strategy.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
kubectl apply -f infra/postgres/cluster-with-backup.yaml
# cluster-with-backup.yaml sets:
#   backup.barmanObjectStore.destinationPath: s3://yarova-pg-backup
#   backup.barmanObjectStore.wal.compression: gzip
#   scheduledBackup: "0 3 * * *" (daily at 03:00 UTC)
clusterComponents.installNote: CNPG backup is configured in the Cluster CRD, not a separate Helm chart:
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Requires S3 write access via IRSA. The CNPG Cluster object must be updated (not a separate install). Velero backs up the Cluster CRD object; the actual data is in S3 via CNPG.
clusterComponents.verifyCommand:
kubectl get scheduledbackup -n postgres
kubectl get backup -n postgres --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp | tail -3
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: most recent backup shows phase=completed

failures.trigger: S3 bucket deleted or CNPG IAM role loses write permission
failures.system: WAL archival fails silently; database continues serving queries; CNPG logs show archival errors
failures.userSees: kubectl describe cluster -n postgres shows "WAL archiving failing" in status conditions
failures.userCan: Fix S3 permission; CNPG will resume archival and catch up on buffered WAL segments automatically

decision.chosen: Decision: CNPG WAL archival to S3, 7-day PITR window
decision.rejected: Rejected: PVC snapshots as the sole database backup — snapshots are not crash-consistent for PostgreSQL; risk of data corruption on restore

clusterComponents.name: OpenCost
clusterComponents.label: OpenCost
clusterComponents.what: Real-time cost allocation tool that breaks down the cloud bill by namespace, service, label, and team — visible in Grafana and queryable via API.
clusterComponents.why: Without OpenCost, the AWS/GCP bill is one number per month. You cannot tell whether the $4,000 spike was caused by the new ML service in prod or a runaway dev namespace.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
helm repo add opencost https://opencost.github.io/opencost-helm-chart
helm upgrade --install opencost opencost/opencost \
--namespace opencost --create-namespace \
--set opencost.exporter.cloudProviderApiKey="" \
--version 1.38.1
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Reads Prometheus metrics for CPU/memory usage (requires Prometheus to be running). Uses cloud pricing APIs to calculate cost per resource unit. Grafana dashboard shows cost breakdown by namespace and label.
clusterComponents.verifyCommand:
kubectl port-forward svc/opencost -n opencost 9090:9090
curl http://localhost:9090/allocation?window=1d | jq '.data[0] | keys'
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: JSON with namespace names and cost breakdowns

failures.trigger: OpenCost cannot reach the cloud pricing API (network policy blocks egress)
failures.system: OpenCost falls back to default on-demand pricing estimates; actual cost data is unavailable
failures.userSees: Grafana cost dashboard shows estimates marked "(estimate)" rather than actuals
failures.userCan: Allow opencost namespace egress to pricing endpoints in network policy; restart OpenCost pod

decision.chosen: Decision: OpenCost over Kubecost
decision.rejected: Rejected: Kubecost — OpenCost is the open-source core of Kubecost; enterprise Kubecost adds a $2,000+/year license for features (governance, anomaly detection) not needed at this scale

clusterComponents.name: Terraform — Cluster Provisioning
clusterComponents.label: Terraform — Cluster Provisioning
clusterComponents.what: Infrastructure-as-code tool that creates and manages the Kubernetes cluster itself (EKS/GKE/AKS), node groups, VPC networking, IAM roles, and cloud resources from .tf files in Git.
clusterComponents.why: Without Terraform, cluster creation is a series of console clicks that cannot be reproduced exactly. A new environment takes days. With Terraform, a new cluster is terraform apply — identical to the existing ones.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
cd infra/clusters/terraform/prod
cp terraform.tfvars.example terraform.tfvars
# Edit: set region, account_id, node_count, node_type
terraform init
terraform apply
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Terraform creates the cluster; all other components (cert-manager, ArgoCD, etc.) are installed into the cluster via the Helm install commands documented in each component above (run in the dependency order shown in the Full Install Sequence section below) after terraform apply completes. Terraform state stored in Component 28.
clusterComponents.verifyCommand:
terraform show | grep cluster_name
kubectl get nodes
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: nodes in Ready state matching the node_count in terraform.tfvars

failures.trigger: terraform apply fails mid-run due to cloud API throttling or quota exceeded
failures.system: Terraform partially creates resources; state file records what was created; next apply completes remaining resources
failures.userSees: terraform apply exits with error; running terraform apply again resolves partial state
failures.userCan: Run terraform apply again — Terraform is idempotent; re-running completes the partial apply safely

decision.chosen: Decision: Terraform over Pulumi or cloud-native CLI tools (eksctl, gcloud container clusters create)
decision.rejected: Rejected: eksctl — AWS-only; scripts would need rewriting for GKE and AKS

clusterComponents.name: Terraform State Backend (S3 / GCS)
clusterComponents.label: Terraform State Backend (S3 / GCS)
clusterComponents.what: Remote storage for the Terraform state file — an S3 bucket (EKS) or GCS bucket (GKE) with DynamoDB/GCS locking to prevent concurrent terraform applies from corrupting state.
clusterComponents.why: Without a remote state backend, two developers running terraform apply simultaneously create duplicate resources (two clusters, two VPCs) — neither knows about the other's changes.
clusterComponents.installCommand:
# AWS
aws s3 mb s3://yarova-terraform-state --region us-east-1
aws dynamodb create-table --table-name terraform-lock \
--attribute-definitions AttributeName=LockID,AttributeType=S \
--key-schema AttributeName=LockID,KeyType=HASH \
--billing-mode PAY_PER_REQUEST

# GCP
gcloud storage buckets create gs://yarova-terraform-state \
--uniform-bucket-level-access
clusterComponents.installNote: Must be created before any Terraform runs. Created manually once per team:
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Every Terraform module's backend.tf references this bucket. DynamoDB (AWS) or GCS object locking provides mutual exclusion for concurrent applies.
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: list of managed resources (module.eks.aws_eks_cluster.main, etc.)

failures.trigger: State file locked (previous terraform apply crashed mid-run)
failures.system: All subsequent terraform commands fail with "Error acquiring the state lock"
failures.userSees: terraform apply output: "Error locking state: Error acquiring the state lock"
failures.userCan: Verify the previous run is truly stopped; then terraform force-unlock <lock-id> (use with caution)

decision.chosen: Decision: S3 + DynamoDB state backend (AWS) / GCS with object locking (GCP)
decision.rejected: Rejected: Terraform Cloud remote state — adds external SaaS dependency; free tier is limited; team prefers self-hosted state

clusterComponents.name: Grafana Datasources — Loki + Tempo
clusterComponents.label: Grafana Datasources — Loki + Tempo
clusterComponents.what: Configures Grafana to use Loki as a log datasource and Tempo as a tracing datasource — enabling log search and trace browsing from the Grafana UI without separate dashboards.
clusterComponents.why: Loki and Tempo run and collect data regardless. Without the datasource wiring, they are invisible in Grafana. This is the final 5-minute step that unlocks the full observability stack.
clusterComponents.installCommand: kubectl apply -f infra/monitoring/grafana-datasources.yaml
clusterComponents.wiredTo: Loki (Component 19) and Tempo (Component 20) must be running before datasources are wired. Grafana Operator CRDs required — included in kube-prometheus-stack.
clusterComponents.verifyCommand:
kubectl port-forward svc/kube-prometheus-stack-grafana -n monitoring 3000:80
# Open http://localhost:3000 > Connections > Data Sources
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: Loki and Tempo listed with green "Data source connected and labels found" status

failures.trigger: Loki or Tempo service ClusterIP changes after a reinstall
failures.system: Grafana datasource URL points to old IP; queries return "connection refused"
failures.userSees: Grafana shows "Data source connected but no labels found" or timeout errors in Explore
failures.userCan: kubectl get svc loki -n monitoring to confirm current URL; update datasource URL; kubectl apply -f infra/monitoring/grafana-datasources.yaml

decision.chosen: Decision: GrafanaDatasource CRDs managed in Git, not Grafana UI
decision.rejected: Rejected: Configure datasources via Grafana UI — configuration is lost on Grafana pod restart unless persistence is configured

clusterComponents.name: Backstage Developer Portal
clusterComponents.label: Backstage Developer Portal
clusterComponents.what: Internal developer portal providing a service catalog of all 106 services, TechDocs for browsable documentation, and software templates for scaffolding new services from the CLI.
clusterComponents.why: Without Backstage, developers discover services by searching GitHub or asking teammates. With 106 services across 3 environments, service ownership, runbooks, and onboarding paths are invisible without a catalog.
clusterComponents.installCommand: See backstage/GITHUB-OAUTH-SETUP.md and scripts/setup-backstage.sh
clusterComponents.installNote: Required before running setup: PostgreSQL running, GITHUB_TOKEN set, AUTH_GITHUB_CLIENT_ID and AUTH_GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET set.
clusterComponents.wiredTo: PostgreSQL (Component 14) — Backstage stores catalog state in a PostgreSQL database. GitHub OAuth — all authentication flows through GitHub. catalog-info.yaml files in each of the 106 service repos register the service in the catalog. backstage/catalog/all.yaml is the root catalog file that imports all service registrations.
clusterComponents.verifyExpected: Expected: JSON array of catalog entries — one object per registered service
clusterComponents.healthExpected: Expected: 200 — Backstage login page is served

failures.trigger: Backstage shows "No catalog results" after login
failures.system: Backstage frontend loads but the catalog API returns an empty array; backend is running but catalog ingestion failed
failures.userSees: Empty catalog — no services, no components, no APIs listed in the UI
failures.userCan: Verify backstage/catalog/all.yaml is readable and all catalog-info.yaml files have valid YAML; restart the Backstage backend pod to trigger a fresh catalog sync

failures.trigger: Backstage backend fails to start — PostgreSQL unreachable or required env var missing
failures.system: Backend pod crashes on startup with a database connection error or missing config error; frontend cannot reach the API
failures.userSees: curl http://localhost:7007/api/catalog/entities returns connection refused or HTTP 500
failures.userCan: Verify PostgreSQL is running (Component 14); verify GITHUB_TOKEN, AUTH_GITHUB_CLIENT_ID, AUTH_GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET are all set; check backend pod logs for the exact missing variable name

decision.chosen: Decision: Backstage with PostgreSQL backend over SQLite
decision.rejected: Rejected: SQLite backend — Backstage default for local dev only; data is lost on pod restart; does not support multiple Backstage replicas

gitopsTools.name: ArgoCD
gitopsTools.label: ArgoCD
gitopsTools.what: GitOps controller that watches Git repositories and automatically deploys changes to Kubernetes — reconciling actual cluster state with desired state in Git.
gitopsTools.installCommand:
helm repo add argo https://argoproj.github.io/argo-helm
helm upgrade --install argocd argo/argo-cd \
  --namespace argocd --create-namespace \
  --values infra/argocd/values.yaml \
  --version 6.7.3
gitopsTools.decisionRejected: Flux v2 — no built-in UI, harder to debug sync failures for non-Kubernetes-expert developers

gitopsTools.name: Argo Rollouts
gitopsTools.label: Argo Rollouts
gitopsTools.what: Extends Kubernetes Deployments with canary and blue-green release strategies — sends a small percentage of traffic to the new version and auto-rolls back if error rate or latency exceeds thresholds.
gitopsTools.decisionChosen: Argo Rollouts canary with Prometheus analysis
gitopsTools.decisionRejected: Manual canary via ingress weight annotations — requires human watching metrics and manually adjusting weights; no automatic rollback

gitopsTools.name: Helm
gitopsTools.label: Helm
gitopsTools.what: Packages Kubernetes manifests as charts; ArgoCD runs helm template with per-service values.yaml then diffs against the cluster. Manual deploy uses helm upgrade --install.
gitopsTools.flagsNote: --install creates the release if missing; --values base then --values values.<env>.yaml override; --set image.tag=<sha> takes precedence over all values files; never use latest.

gitopsTools.name: Kustomize
gitopsTools.label: Kustomize
gitopsTools.what: Per-environment config patches layered on top of the base Helm values — lets dev/staging/prod diverge on exactly the fields they need (replicaCount, resources, image tag) while inheriting everything else.
gitopsTools.promotionField: images[].newTag is updated with the validated git SHA per environment.

gitopsTools.name: Flux v2
gitopsTools.label: Flux v2
gitopsTools.what: Considered as the GitOps controller but rejected in favor of ArgoCD.
gitopsTools.whyRejected: Flux v2 — no built-in UI, harder to debug sync failures for non-Kubernetes-expert developers.

invariants.name: I-P0-1
invariants.label: I-P0-1 — No direct push to main ever
invariants.condition: No direct push to main ever
invariants.ifViolated: Unreviewed code ships
invariants.enforcedBy: GitHub branch protection rules

invariants.name: I-P0-2
invariants.label: I-P0-2 — CI uses OIDC tokens — no long-lived secrets stored in CI
invariants.condition: CI uses OIDC tokens — no long-lived secrets stored in CI
invariants.ifViolated: Credential leak if repo compromised
invariants.enforcedBy: GitHub OIDC + cloud IAM

invariants.name: I-P0-3
invariants.label: I-P0-3 — Push scoped to exact repo + branch only
invariants.condition: Push scoped to exact repo + branch only
invariants.ifViolated: Any branch can push any image
invariants.enforcedBy: Registry policy + OIDC role binding

invariants.name: I-P0-4
invariants.label: I-P0-4 — Cluster rejects any image not signed by the pipeline
invariants.condition: Cluster rejects any image not signed by the pipeline
invariants.ifViolated: Unsigned images run in production
invariants.enforcedBy: Kyverno ClusterPolicy

invariants.name: I-P0-5
invariants.label: I-P0-5 — All dependency updates arrive as PRs — never manual edits
invariants.condition: All dependency updates arrive as PRs — never manual edits
invariants.ifViolated: Drift accumulates silently
invariants.enforcedBy: Renovate / Dependabot config

invariants.name: I-P1-1
invariants.label: I-P1-1 — A commit containing a detected secret is blocked before it leaves the developer machine
invariants.condition: A commit containing a detected secret is blocked before it leaves the developer machine
invariants.ifViolated: Secret reaches remote, requires rotation
invariants.enforcedBy: gitleaks pre-commit hook

invariants.name: I-P1-2
invariants.label: I-P1-2 — Dockerfile linting (hadolint) and IaC checks (checkov) block commit on failure
invariants.condition: Dockerfile linting (hadolint) and IaC checks (checkov) block commit on failure
invariants.ifViolated: Bad Dockerfile or IaC ships to PR
invariants.enforcedBy: pre-commit framework + hadolint + checkov

invariants.name: I-P2-1
invariants.label: I-P2-1 — Image is built to tarball only — no push to registry ever
invariants.condition: Image is built to tarball only — no push to registry ever
invariants.ifViolated: Unscanned image in registry
invariants.enforcedBy: Docker build with --output type=tar or no --push

invariants.name: I-P2-2
invariants.label: I-P2-2 — All five scans complete before build step begins
invariants.condition: All five scans complete before build step begins
invariants.ifViolated: Build promotes code that fails a scan
invariants.enforcedBy: CI job ordering — scans in parallel, build depends on all five

invariants.name: I-P2-3
invariants.label: I-P2-3 — Trivy scans the tarball — not a registry image
invariants.condition: Trivy scans the tarball — not a registry image
invariants.ifViolated: A pushed image is scanned; PR gate is bypassed
invariants.enforcedBy: Trivy --input tarball.tar

invariants.name: I-P2-4
invariants.label: I-P2-4 — At least one human approval required before merge
invariants.condition: At least one human approval required before merge
invariants.ifViolated: No human review of code changes
invariants.enforcedBy: GitHub branch protection — required reviewers

invariants.name: I-P2-5
invariants.label: I-P2-5 — SBOM is generated from the same tarball scanned by Trivy
invariants.condition: SBOM is generated from the same tarball scanned by Trivy
invariants.ifViolated: SBOM and scan diverge — different artifact
invariants.enforcedBy: SBOM tool points at the same tarball

invariants.name: I-P3-1
invariants.label: I-P3-1 — Image is signed only after it is pushed and scanned — never before
invariants.condition: Image is signed only after it is pushed and scanned — never before
invariants.ifViolated: Signature covers unscanned image
invariants.enforcedBy: CI job ordering — sign depends on container scan

invariants.name: I-P3-2
invariants.label: I-P3-2 — SBOM attestation is attached only after signing — never before
invariants.condition: SBOM attestation is attached only after signing — never before
invariants.ifViolated: Attestation has no verified image to anchor to
invariants.enforcedBy: CI job ordering — attest depends on sign

invariants.name: I-P3-3
invariants.label: I-P3-3 — Trivy scans the registry image digest — not a tarball or tag
invariants.condition: Trivy scans the registry image digest — not a tarball or tag
invariants.ifViolated: Scan and deployed image diverge
invariants.enforcedBy: Trivy --image registry/repo@sha256:...

invariants.name: I-P3-4
invariants.label: I-P3-4 — SLSA provenance is generated only after image is signed
invariants.condition: SLSA provenance is generated only after image is signed
invariants.ifViolated: Provenance anchors to an unsigned image
invariants.enforcedBy: CI job ordering — SLSA depends on sign

invariants.name: I-P3-5
invariants.label: I-P3-5 — Semantic version tag applied before SBOM and SLSA generation
invariants.condition: Semantic version tag applied before SBOM and SLSA generation
invariants.ifViolated: SBOM and SLSA reference untagged digest
invariants.enforcedBy: CI job ordering — tag before attest and provenance

invariants.name: I-P3-6
invariants.label: I-P3-6 — All tests pass before DAST runs
invariants.condition: All tests pass before DAST runs
invariants.ifViolated: DAST runs against a broken application
invariants.enforcedBy: CI job ordering — DAST depends on test success

invariants.name: I-R-1
invariants.label: I-R-1 — No image is promoted from registry to any environment without cosign signature verification
invariants.condition: No image is promoted from registry to any environment without cosign signature verification
invariants.ifViolated: Unsigned image runs in environment
invariants.enforcedBy: cosign verify at registry promotion step

invariants.name: I-R-2
invariants.label: I-R-2 — SBOM is attached to the registry image before any promotion
invariants.condition: SBOM is attached to the registry image before any promotion
invariants.ifViolated: Environment has no SBOM for the running image
invariants.enforcedBy: OCI SBOM attach step before promotion gate

invariants.name: I-R-3
invariants.label: I-R-3 — SLSA provenance is attached to the registry image before any promotion
invariants.condition: SLSA provenance is attached to the registry image before any promotion
invariants.ifViolated: Supply chain attestation missing in environment
invariants.enforcedBy: OCI SLSA attach step before promotion gate

invariants.name: I-PR-1
invariants.label: I-PR-1 — Signature verified in the target environment before ArgoCD / Flux deploys
invariants.condition: Signature verified in the target environment before ArgoCD / Flux deploys
invariants.ifViolated: Deployment of unverified image
invariants.enforcedBy: cosign verify step in promotion workflow
invariants.phase: Promotions — all 4 environments (DEV / TEST / STAGING / PROD)

invariants.name: I-PR-2
invariants.label: I-PR-2 — Deployment references image by digest — never by mutable tag
invariants.condition: Deployment references image by digest — never by mutable tag
invariants.ifViolated: Tag re-tag swaps running image silently
invariants.enforcedBy: GitOps PR sets image: repo@sha256:...
invariants.phase: Promotions — all 4 environments (DEV / TEST / STAGING / PROD)

invariants.name: I-PR-3
invariants.label: I-PR-3 — Promotion to the next environment requires explicit gate approval
invariants.condition: Promotion to the next environment requires explicit gate approval
invariants.ifViolated: Any environment auto-promotes on test pass
invariants.enforcedBy: Manual approval gate or policy gate in ArgoCD
invariants.phase: Promotions — all 4 environments (DEV / TEST / STAGING / PROD)

invariants.name: I-PR-4
invariants.label: I-PR-4 — Tests must pass before gate opens — gate cannot open on partial test results
invariants.condition: Tests must pass before gate opens — gate cannot open on partial test results
invariants.ifViolated: Broken build promotes through environments
invariants.enforcedBy: CI gate condition — all test steps green
invariants.phase: Promotions — all 4 environments (DEV / TEST / STAGING / PROD)

conceptNotes.title: 00 — Getting Started
conceptNotes.body: What this covers: What pipeline-studio is, who it is for, and how all 8 build axes and 106 services fit together. The mental model every maintainer must have before touching anything. When to read: Before reading any other doc. Before your first commit. When onboarding a new team member.

conceptNotes.title: Pipeline Schema
conceptNotes.body: What this covers: The 6-phase CI/CD pipeline — every phase, every stage, trigger conditions, outputs, and why each phase boundary exists. When to read: Start here to understand the CI/CD pipeline shape. When building a new service, read 01-framework-catalog.html first.

conceptNotes.title: Stage Types
conceptNotes.body: What this covers: All 44 pipeline stages classified as DevOps or DevSecOps — who owns a failure when a stage fails. When to read: A stage failed and you need to know who owns it. Understanding the difference between a workflow error and a security gate.

conceptNotes.title: Maintainability Runbook
conceptNotes.body: What this covers: Step-by-step checklists for every maintenance task — adding a new doc, updating versions, updating market data, keeping nav in sync. File map for all 19 docs. When to read: Before adding a new doc, running a version update batch, or re-verifying any data in any doc.

conceptNotes.title: 20 — Local Development
conceptNotes.body: What this covers: How to run any of the 106 services locally. Prerequisites, one-command setup, verify steps, and failure modes for every tool. When to read: Before writing any code. When setting up a new machine. When a service fails to start locally.

conceptNotes.title: 22 — Add a Service
conceptNotes.body: What this covers: Complete step-by-step guide to add a new service to pipeline-studio. From framework choice to Backstage catalog entry. Every command is copy-pasteable. When to read: When a new framework or service type needs to be added. When a customer requests support for a specific stack.

conceptNotes.title: 24 — Debugging
conceptNotes.body: What this covers: How to diagnose and fix any failure in pipeline-studio. Symptoms, diagnosis commands, root causes, and fixes for every known failure mode. When to read: When a service fails to start. When a pod crashes. When CI fails. When ArgoCD shows an error. When a build fails.

conceptNotes.title: Deployment environments — dev / staging / prod
conceptNotes.body: Three environments promote in order: dev -> staging -> prod. Each is a separate Git path that ArgoCD watches independently. replicaCount: dev 1, staging 2, prod 3+. resources.requests.cpu: dev 50m, staging 100m, prod 200m. resources.limits.memory: dev 256Mi, staging 512Mi, prod 1Gi. image.tag: dev latest dev SHA, staging validated SHA, prod release SHA. Each environment points to a different commit, enabling staged promotion.

conceptNotes.title: Image promotion — Git is the source of truth
conceptNotes.body: git push -> GitHub Actions builds and pushes image -> GHCR stores it -> developer edits values.yaml / kustomize overlay with the new SHA -> ArgoCD detects the Git diff and syncs within 3 minutes -> Helm chart applied -> Kubernetes rolling update -> pod running. Image tag is the git SHA, never :latest, because :latest is mutable and breaks rollback. ArgoCD is the only actor that applies changes to the cluster; developers do not run kubectl apply in normal operation.

conceptNotes.title: Rollback — three options
conceptNotes.body: Option A Helm rollback: <60 seconds, NOT Git source of truth (cluster drifts until ArgoCD syncs), Helm history only; use when the cluster is on fire and follow up with C immediately. Option B Git revert: ~3 minutes (ArgoCD sync), Git is source of truth from the first second, full git log audit trail; preferred in all non-emergency cases. Option C Pin previous tag: ~3 minutes, Git source of truth, explicit rollback commit message; use when you want a clear commit naming the SHA rather than a generic revert commit.

conceptNotes.title: GitOps — what ArgoCD watches
conceptNotes.body: ArgoCD watches two Git paths, both on a 3-minute polling interval: infra/argocd/ controls ArgoCD Application definitions (which apps exist and which cluster/namespace they deploy to); services/*/helm/values.yaml controls per-service Helm values including image.tag. Separate Application resources exist per environment, so updating the staging overlay only affects staging.

apiGateways.name: Kong
apiGateways.notes: OSS + enterprise; plugin auth, rate limiting, mTLS.

apiGateways.name: Google Apigee
apiGateways.notes: Enterprise API mgmt; OAuth2, quotas, analytics.

apiGateways.name: AWS API Gateway
apiGateways.notes: Managed; IAM/Cognito auth, WAF, usage plans.

apiGateways.name: NGINX Gateway
apiGateways.notes: Ingress-level; JWT auth, mTLS, rate limit.

phases.name: Promotions — DEV → TEST → STAGING → PROD (same pattern for all frameworks)

stages.command: Sync from dev overlay; wait for rollout
Run integration test suite against deployed service
Required approvers listed in environment settings
Auto-rollback if error rate >1% or p99 >2s

envInstructions.text: SCA note: Replace npm with yarn audit or pnpm audit per ARG PM
envInstructions.label: SCA note:
envInstructions.instruction: Replace npm with yarn audit or pnpm audit per ARG PM

envInstructions.text: Build note: ARG PM=pnpm → pnpm install --frozen-lockfile && pnpm run build
envInstructions.label: Build note:
envInstructions.instruction: ARG PM=pnpm → pnpm install --frozen-lockfile && pnpm run build

phases.Phase 0: Confirm registry supports signed FIPS images
phases.Phase 2: Build with --target runtime-fips in FIPS-capable builder
phases.Phase 3: Push both runtime and runtime-fips tags; sign both with cosign
phases.Registry: Store both :$SHA and :$SHA-fips tags; sign-verify both
phases.Promotions: Deploy runtime-fips image to all environments; enforce in Kyverno policy

phases.Phase 0: Restrict registry write to release branch only; enable audit logging
phases.Phase 2: Fail on any CRITICAL CVE (Trivy --exit-code 1 --severity CRITICAL); fail on hardcoded secrets
phases.Phase 3: Require zero CRITICAL CVEs before sign; add payment-specific SAST rules
phases.Registry: Enable immutable tags; log all pull events; restrict pull to production namespaces
phases.Promotions: Network segmentation policy in Kyverno; no internet egress from payment containers

phases.Phase 0: Enable audit logging on registry; document BAA with cloud provider
phases.Phase 2: Fail on HIGH+CRITICAL CVEs; scan for PHI patterns in source code
phases.Phase 3: Enforce TLS 1.2+ in app config; non-root USER; read-only filesystem where possible
phases.Registry: Enable access logging; restrict pull to HIPAA-scoped namespaces
phases.Promotions: Enforce Pod Security Standards (restricted); seccompProfile RuntimeDefault

complianceDeltas.appliesTo: US federal agencies and SaaS providers

phases.Phase 0: OIDC trust to FedRAMP-authorized cloud region; FIPS registry access only
phases.Phase 2: FIPS build target required; SBOM in SPDX 2.3 format; SLSA Level 3 attestation
phases.Phase 3: Container must use FIPS-certified runtime; cosign sign with hardware key or Fulcio
phases.Registry: Deploy to GovCloud (AWS us-gov-* / Azure USGov); immutable tags enforced
phases.Promotions: Continuous monitoring via AWS Config / Azure Policy; runtime image scanned daily

phases.Phase 0: MFA on all CI/CD accounts; OIDC for keyless auth; CODEOWNERS for all paths
phases.Phase 2: Access control: only named reviewers can approve PRs containing CUI
phases.Phase 3: Full audit trail: every pipeline run logged, signed, and retained 3 years
phases.Registry: Restrict to US-only regions; no cross-region replication to foreign jurisdictions
phases.Promotions: Incident response plan documented; deployment events logged to SIEM

phases.Phase 0: Enable branch protection evidence collection; log all admin actions
phases.Phase 2: Evidence: save scan results as artifacts for audit evidence packages
phases.Phase 3: Retain all pipeline artifacts (logs, SBOMs, scan results) for audit period
phases.Registry: Enable pull logging; image retention policy documented
phases.Promotions: Change management: every deployment linked to approved ticket; rollback tested

phases.Phase 0: Segregation of duties: developer cannot approve own PR or deploy to production
phases.Phase 2: Required reviewer must be from a separate team (enforce via CODEOWNERS)
phases.Phase 3: Change management ticket required before merge to main; approval documented
phases.Registry: Immutable image tags; no manual overwrites in production registry
phases.Promotions: PROD deployment requires second-person authorization; audit log exported to GRC tool

phases.Phase 0: Confirm all CI/CD infra in EU regions (GitHub Actions self-hosted or EU data center)
phases.Phase 2: Scan source for hardcoded PII; fail on any PII found in code or config
phases.Phase 3: Log processing activities in image labels; ensure encryption at rest enforced
phases.Registry: Registry must be in EU region; cross-border transfer documented
phases.Promotions: Data residency enforced in Kubernetes namespace policy; egress to non-EU blocked

phases.Phase 0: Confirm all CI/CD infra in Canadian regions (AWS ca-central-1 / Azure Canada Central)
phases.Phase 2: Scan source for Canadian PII patterns; fail on any found
phases.Phase 3: Ensure image metadata documents data processing purpose
phases.Registry: Registry must be in Canadian region; no personal data in image layers
phases.Promotions: Namespace policy enforces ca-central-1 / Canada Central regions only

phases.Phase 0: Air-gap CI from internet where possible; strict access control with role-based auth
phases.Phase 2: Fail on any CRITICAL CVE; no internet access during build (vendored deps)
phases.Phase 3: Offline build environment; all deps vendored; no external network calls in build
phases.Registry: Air-gapped private registry; no public registry pull
phases.Promotions: Change control ticket with CIP change management evidence; rollback plan documented

phases.Phase 0: ISMS asset register: document all pipeline components and their risk ratings
phases.Phase 2: Risk-based scan thresholds: document risk acceptance for deferred vulnerabilities
phases.Phase 3: Full artifact retention per retention schedule (usually 3–5 years)
phases.Registry: Access review quarterly; registry credentials rotated annually
phases.Promotions: Incident response runbook linked from deployment pipeline; change approval documented

phases.name: Phase 0 — Bootstrap (once, same for all frameworks — see global section below)

stages.tool: GitHub OIDC + cloud IAM / Workload Identity

Scope push permission to exact repo + branch only; pull allowed from all
